item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Regioselective Cyclocarbonylation of Alkynols: A Mechanism Study
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Density functional theory (DFT) was used to investigate the mechanism of regioselective cyclocarbonylation of alkynols catalyzed by palladium. Our study provides insights into the Pd active species oxidation states and reaction pathways. Distortion/interaction–activation strain (D/I‐AS) analyses were used to elucidate the role of ligand and the interaction energy in controlling stereoselectivity.
α‐Alkylene‐β‐lactone is an important skeleton in a variety of biologically active molecules. It is of great significance to study the mechanism of regioselective cyclocarbonylation of tertiary alkynols catalyzed by palladium, which is applied to the synthesis of α‐alkylene‐β‐lactone. We employed density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the M06‐2X level to investigate the valence state of Pd participating in the catalytic cycle, the reaction pathways, and the mechanism of ligand‐controlled regioselectivity. Our results reveal that palladium participates in the reaction with the Pd(II) oxidation state. Distortion/interaction–activation strain(D/I‐AS) analyses indicate that the regioselectivity of the reaction with ligand L1 is predominantly governed by hydrogen–bond interaction in alkyne migration insertion, while the regioselectivity of the reaction with ligand L2 is mainly governed by kinetically suppressing byproduct formation.
item.content:encoded:
Density functional theory (DFT) was used to investigate the mechanism of regioselective cyclocarbonylation of alkynols catalyzed by palladium. Our study provides insights into the Pd active species oxidation states and reaction pathways. Distortion/interaction–activation strain (D/I-AS) analyses were used to elucidate the role of ligand and the interaction energy in controlling stereoselectivity.


α-Alkylene-β-lactone is an important skeleton in a variety of biologically active molecules. It is of great significance to study the mechanism of regioselective cyclocarbonylation of tertiary alkynols catalyzed by palladium, which is applied to the synthesis of α-alkylene-β-lactone. We employed density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the M06-2X level to investigate the valence state of Pd participating in the catalytic cycle, the reaction pathways, and the mechanism of ligand-controlled regioselectivity. Our results reveal that palladium participates in the reaction with the Pd(II) oxidation state. Distortion/interaction–activation strain(D/I-AS) analyses indicate that the regioselectivity of the reaction with ligand L1 is predominantly governed by hydrogen–bond interaction in alkyne migration insertion, while the regioselectivity of the reaction with ligand L2 is mainly governed by kinetically suppressing byproduct formation.
item.dc:creator: Kaili Xie, Fengyue Zhao, Peng Dai, Qing Xia, Fang Liu, Weihua Zhang, Kendall N. Houk
item.dc:title: Regioselective Cyclocarbonylation of Alkynols: A Mechanism Study

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chemoselectivity and Enantioselectivity in the Conjugate Reduction of Cinnamate Esters and a Tandem Conjugate Reduction‐Ester Hydrogenation Using Manganese Catalysts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
An improved synthesis of a manganese catalyst used for hydrogenation of ketones, esters and imines is reported. The catalyst has now been found to effect tandem conjugate reduction‐ester hydrogenations with very low amounts of allylic alcohol by‐products. At low temperatures the reactions give mainly conjugate reduction product without C=O reduction including an example of enantioselective alkene hydrogenation catalyzed by manganese.
An improved synthesis of manganese(I) tricarbonyl complexes of the Phosphino‐Ferrocenyl‐Amino‐Methyl‐Pyridine (PFAMPy) ligand family is reported. A comparison of both the neutral form [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)2Br] and the cationic form [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)3]Br was made for an enantioselective ketone hydrogenation, with both catalysts giving high yields and enantiomer ratios over 98:2. The [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)3]Br catalyst was then applied in tandem conjugate reduction‐ester hydrogenation to convert cinnamate esters into aryl propanols. This could be achieved for disubstituted cinnamates with the problematic inseparable allyl alcohol side products almost eliminated below 0.5%. A strategy to prevent CO bond reduction preceding CC reduction, and hence allylic alcohol side products, is to use a tert‐butyl ester and mild conditions for the first few hours of reaction, prior to increasing temperature to promote ester hydrogenation. This approach is needed for trisubstituted cinnamate esters, which otherwise give mixtures. It is possible to carry out just conjugate reductions to saturated esters at lower temperatures without significant ester hydrogenation. Examples of manganese‐catalyzed asymmetric hydrogenation of alkenes are presented in the form of an enantioselective and chemoselective conjugate reduction of trisubstituted cinnamate esters.
item.content:encoded:

At low temperatures the reactions give mainly conjugate reduction product without C=O reduction including an example of enantioselective alkene hydrogenation catalyzed by manganese.


An improved synthesis of manganese(I) tricarbonyl complexes of the Phosphino-Ferrocenyl-Amino-Methyl-Pyridine (PFAMPy) ligand family is reported. A comparison of both the neutral form [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)2Br] and the cationic form [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)3]Br was made for an enantioselective ketone hydrogenation, with both catalysts giving high yields and enantiomer ratios over 98:2. The [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)3]Br catalyst was then applied in tandem conjugate reduction-ester hydrogenation to convert cinnamate esters into aryl propanols. This could be achieved for disubstituted cinnamates with the problematic inseparable allyl alcohol side products almost eliminated below 0.5%. A strategy to prevent CO bond reduction preceding CC reduction, and hence allylic alcohol side products, is to use a tert-butyl ester and mild conditions for the first few hours of reaction, prior to increasing temperature to promote ester hydrogenation. This approach is needed for trisubstituted cinnamate esters, which otherwise give mixtures. Examples of manganese-catalyzed asymmetric hydrogenation of alkenes are presented in the form of an enantioselective and chemoselective conjugate reduction of trisubstituted cinnamate esters.
item.dc:creator: José A. Fuentes, Matthew L. Clarke
item.dc:title: Chemoselectivity and Enantioselectivity in the Conjugate Reduction of Cinnamate Esters and a Tandem Conjugate Reduction‐Ester Hydrogenation Using Manganese Catalysts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chiral Isonitriles‐Iron‐Cyclopentadienone Complexes for Enantioselective Transfer Hydrogenation of Ketones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Inserting isonitriles ligands on a chiral cyclopentadienone complex, upon light activation, chiral catalysts efficiently performed under mild conditions transfer hydrogenation of ketones using isopropanol. The resulting alcohols were obtained in up to 78:22 er, confirming the potential of such iron complexes.
By substituting one CO ligand with an isonitrile, we have developed a novel class of chiral iron cyclopentadienone complexes that are active in transfer hydrogenation under photoactivation. These complexes display high reactivity across a broad range of substrates, achieving enantiomeric ratios of up to 78:22. We explored various strategies for introducing chirality into the iron complex, including the use of chiral cyclopentadienone ligands, chiral isonitrile ligands, and combinations of both. Upon light irradiation, CO dissociation renders the iron center stereogenic, resulting in unique chiral molecules with multiple stereogenic elements. Notably, these iron complexes efficiently reduce ketones under mild conditions using isopropanol as a hydrogen donor, eliminating the need for high hydrogen pressures typically required for such systems. Insights into the role of the chiral cyclopentadienone ligand offer promising directions for designing more selective chiral iron catalysts.
item.content:encoded:
Inserting isonitriles ligands on a chiral cyclopentadienone complex, upon light activation, chiral catalysts efficiently performed under mild conditions transfer hydrogenation of ketones using isopropanol. The resulting alcohols were obtained in up to 78:22 er, confirming the potential of such iron complexes.


By substituting one CO ligand with an isonitrile, we have developed a novel class of chiral iron cyclopentadienone complexes that are active in transfer hydrogenation under photoactivation. These complexes display high reactivity across a broad range of substrates, achieving enantiomeric ratios of up to 78:22. We explored various strategies for introducing chirality into the iron complex, including the use of chiral cyclopentadienone ligands, chiral isonitrile ligands, and combinations of both. Upon light irradiation, CO dissociation renders the iron center stereogenic, resulting in unique chiral molecules with multiple stereogenic elements. Notably, these iron complexes efficiently reduce ketones under mild conditions using isopropanol as a hydrogen donor, eliminating the need for high hydrogen pressures typically required for such systems. Insights into the role of the chiral cyclopentadienone ligand offer promising directions for designing more selective chiral iron catalysts.
item.dc:creator: Gaëtan Quintil, Jacques Pécaut, Ragnar Bjornsson, Amélie Kochem, Adrien Quintard
item.dc:title: Chiral Isonitriles‐Iron‐Cyclopentadienone Complexes for Enantioselective Transfer Hydrogenation of Ketones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Light Driven Acid and Base Controlled Selective Synthesis of 3,4‐Dihydroquinoxalinones and Quinoxalinones via Cascade Cyclization of Aryl Diazoacetates With o‐Phenylenediamines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
A visible‐light‐induced, photocatalyst‐free protocol for the selective synthesis of 3,4‐dihydroquinoxalinones and quinoxalinones has been developed. The cascade cyclization of phenyldiazoacetates with o‐phenylenediamines, promoted by a Bronsted acid/weak base catalytic system, enables efficient CN bond formation. This green methodology features a broad substrate scope and provides access to diverse quinoxalinone derivatives.
Herein, we report a direct, visible light‐induced synthesis of quinoxalinones from diazoacetates and o‐phenylenediamines under mild reaction conditions. This versatile protocol enables the controlled selective synthesis of 3,4‐dihydroquinoxalinones or quinoxalinones by tuning of acid–base conditions. In mechanistic studies, both singlet oxygen (1O2) generation and single‐electron transfer plays a crucial role in quinoxalinones synthesis. The gram‐scale synthesis of 3,4‐dihydroquinoxalinone and quinoxalinone affords a moderate yield, which proves the practicability of the reaction conditions.
item.content:encoded:
A visible-light-induced, photocatalyst-free protocol for the selective synthesis of 3,4-dihydroquinoxalinones and quinoxalinones has been developed. The cascade cyclization of phenyldiazoacetates with o-phenylenediamines, promoted by a Bronsted acid/weak base catalytic system, enables efficient CN bond formation. This green methodology features a broad substrate scope and provides access to diverse quinoxalinone derivatives.


Herein, we report a direct, visible light-induced synthesis of quinoxalinones from diazoacetates and o-phenylenediamines under mild reaction conditions. This versatile protocol enables the controlled selective synthesis of 3,4-dihydroquinoxalinones or quinoxalinones by tuning of acid–base conditions. In mechanistic studies, both singlet oxygen (1O2) generation and single-electron transfer plays a crucial role in quinoxalinones synthesis. The gram-scale synthesis of 3,4-dihydroquinoxalinone and quinoxalinone affords a moderate yield, which proves the practicability of the reaction conditions.
item.dc:creator: Kesavan Prasanth, Pakkirisamy Nithishkumar, Ramasamy Anandhan
item.dc:title: Light Driven Acid and Base Controlled Selective Synthesis of 3,4‐Dihydroquinoxalinones and Quinoxalinones via Cascade Cyclization of Aryl Diazoacetates With o‐Phenylenediamines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Unveiling Reaction Pathways in the Alkaline Oxidative Conversion of Guaiacyl Lignin Into Low‐Molecular‐Weight Phenols From β‐Ether Linkages
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Vanillin is an industrial platform chemical produced via the alkaline oxidative degradation of lignin in wood. Our approaches, including reaction tracing with isotopic fingerprinting, indicate that vanillin forms through multiple pathways originating at both phenolic and nonphenolic sites. The by‐products vanillic acid and acetoguaiacone arise primarily from nonphenolic sites.
The oxidative degradation of lignin, a major component of lignocellulose, in alkaline media is important in both academic and applied research, serving as a fundamental approach for lignin analysis and chemical conversion. Although it is well known that the alkaline oxidation commonly yields benzaldehydes, benzoic acids, and acetophenones, the pathways underlying their formation remain insufficiently understood. This study focuses on the β‐ether, the most abundant interunit linkage in lignin, and investigates the formation pathways of vanillin, vanillic acid, and acetoguaiacone from guaiacyl lignin in 4.0 mol/L NaOH aq. at 120°C under O2. By examining the degradation behavior of a lignin model polymer LP composed exclusively of β‐ether, along with various dimeric models, we found that vanillin is mainly generated through a sequential reaction initiated from phenolic terminal units. Another vanillin formation pathway originating from nonphenolic internal units contributed to the overall oxidation process to some extent. Vanillic acid and acetoguaiacone were formed via pathways distinct from that of vanillin, with their formation largely attributed to reactions involving internal units. Further investigation of the degradation behavior of isolated lignin revealed that the contribution of the sequential vanillin formation pathway from phenolic terminals was lower than that observed for LP.
item.content:encoded:
Vanillin is an industrial platform chemical produced via the alkaline oxidative degradation of lignin in wood. Our approaches, including reaction tracing with isotopic fingerprinting, indicate that vanillin forms through multiple pathways originating at both phenolic and nonphenolic sites. The by-products vanillic acid and acetoguaiacone arise primarily from nonphenolic sites.


The oxidative degradation of lignin, a major component of lignocellulose, in alkaline media is important in both academic and applied research, serving as a fundamental approach for lignin analysis and chemical conversion. Although it is well known that the alkaline oxidation commonly yields benzaldehydes, benzoic acids, and acetophenones, the pathways underlying their formation remain insufficiently understood. This study focuses on the β-ether, the most abundant interunit linkage in lignin, and investigates the formation pathways of vanillin, vanillic acid, and acetoguaiacone from guaiacyl lignin in 4.0 mol/L NaOH aq. at 120°C under O2. By examining the degradation behavior of a lignin model polymer LP composed exclusively of β-ether, along with various dimeric models, we found that vanillin is mainly generated through a sequential reaction initiated from phenolic terminal units. Another vanillin formation pathway originating from nonphenolic internal units contributed to the overall oxidation process to some extent. Vanillic acid and acetoguaiacone were formed via pathways distinct from that of vanillin, with their formation largely attributed to reactions involving internal units. Further investigation of the degradation behavior of isolated lignin revealed that the contribution of the sequential vanillin formation pathway from phenolic terminals was lower than that observed for LP.
item.dc:creator: Ayami Ishikawa, Haruka Shimizu, Takashi Hosoya, Hisashi Miyafuji
item.dc:title: Unveiling Reaction Pathways in the Alkaline Oxidative Conversion of Guaiacyl Lignin Into Low‐Molecular‐Weight Phenols From β‐Ether Linkages

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Functionalized 10(2,4,6‐Triarylphenyl)‐10H‐phenothiazines Synthesized Without Metal Catalysts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
New sterically highly congested triaryl phenyl phenothiazines are obtained via Zimmermann–Fischer condensation and structurally investigated by single crystal X‐ray analysis.
A new synthetic approach to functionalized N‐(2,4,6‐triarylphenyl) phenothiazines is presented. The methodology is based on a modified Fischer–Zimmermann condensation of triaryl pyrylium salts with aryl acetates, specifically sodium β‐N‐phenothiazine acetate, and affords sterically highly congested compounds. Single‐crystal X‐ray analyses of several of these compounds reveal that the pronounced steric hindrance results in an almost perpendicular orientation between the triaryl phenyl moiety ring and the phenothiazine core, accompanied by a significantly reduced folding angle of the latter. These structural arrangements also correlate with the low oxidation potential of the compounds.
item.content:encoded:
New sterically highly congested triaryl phenyl phenothiazines are obtained via Zimmermann–Fischer condensation and structurally investigated by single crystal X-ray analysis.


A new synthetic approach to functionalized N-(2,4,6-triarylphenyl) phenothiazines is presented. The methodology is based on a modified Fischer–Zimmermann condensation of triaryl pyrylium salts with aryl acetates, specifically sodium β-N-phenothiazine acetate, and affords sterically highly congested compounds. Single-crystal X-ray analyses of several of these compounds reveal that the pronounced steric hindrance results in an almost perpendicular orientation between the triaryl phenyl moiety ring and the phenothiazine core, accompanied by a significantly reduced folding angle of the latter. These structural arrangements also correlate with the low oxidation potential of the compounds.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Rebecca Wentzke, Ute Müller, Anh Stummer, Andreas Schneider, Gregor Schnakenburg, Pierre Pascal Kachel, Inke Siewert, Sigurd Höger
item.dc:title: Functionalized 10(2,4,6‐Triarylphenyl)‐10H‐phenothiazines Synthesized Without Metal Catalysts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Additives Promote Divergent Reactivity in Photolytic Deoxygenation and Lactonization Reactions of Cobalt Alkoxycarbonyls
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
We report a carbonylation‐homolysis process to access acyl radicals that can undergo decarboxylation or cyclization onto tethered alkenes. In both cases, ligands and additives are shown to influence the outcome to give saturated or unsaturated products, in some cases with isomerization of the initial products. We describe mechanistic experiments providing evidence of cobalt hydrides and the application toward a bioactive lactone natural product.
The formation of lactones via radical‐mediated CC bond formation is a powerful but overlooked method, especially given recent interest in the conversion of alcohols to radical intermediates using photochemistry. Building on our previous work with cobalt porphyrin complexes, a carbonylation‐homolysis process is described for complex unsaturated alcohols and a simple benzylic model system, leading to the production of saturated or unsaturated products. Ligands and additives are demonstrated to promote either reductive or oxidative termination, leading to divergent reactivity in different contexts. The role of cobalt hydride intermediates is also discussed, as well as the application of this strategy toward the synthesis of a lactone‐containing limonoid natural product.
item.content:encoded:
We report a carbonylation-homolysis process to access acyl radicals that can undergo decarboxylation or cyclization onto tethered alkenes. In both cases, ligands and additives are shown to influence the outcome to give saturated or unsaturated products, in some cases with isomerization of the initial products. We describe mechanistic experiments providing evidence of cobalt hydrides and the application toward a bioactive lactone natural product.


The formation of lactones via radical-mediated CC bond formation is a powerful but overlooked method, especially given recent interest in the conversion of alcohols to radical intermediates using photochemistry. Building on our previous work with cobalt porphyrin complexes, a carbonylation-homolysis process is described for complex unsaturated alcohols and a simple benzylic model system, leading to the production of saturated or unsaturated products. Ligands and additives are demonstrated to promote either reductive or oxidative termination, leading to divergent reactivity in different contexts. The role of cobalt hydride intermediates is also discussed, as well as the application of this strategy toward the synthesis of a lactone-containing limonoid natural product.
item.dc:creator: Jacob N. Hackbarth, Dana R. Chambers, Cory T. Ludwig, Tyler K. Brown, David B. C. Martin
item.dc:title: Additives Promote Divergent Reactivity in Photolytic Deoxygenation and Lactonization Reactions of Cobalt Alkoxycarbonyls

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Brønsted Acid–Driven C6‐Selective Sulfonylation of Coumarins Promoted by Cu(OTf)2: Synthesis of Coumarin–Sulfone Scaffolds
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description: A Cu(OTf)2‐mediated protocol for the regioselective CH sulfonylation of coumarin derivatives with arylsulfonyl chlorides is reported. This transformation exhibits broad substrate scope and excellent functional‐group tolerance, affording a wide range of C6‐sulfonylated coumarins in moderate to excellent yields. The methodology also extends to heteroaromatic frameworks, such as xanthone, and to bioactive scaffolds exemplified by methoxsalane, enabling selective CH sulfonylation even at unconventional positions. Mechanistic investigations, supported by DFT calculations and Fukui function analysis, reveal that transient protonation of coumarin by in situ–generated triflic acid significantly enhances electrophilic susceptibility at the C6 position, thus overriding the intrinsic C3 reactivity of the neutral scaffold. Computational results favor an electrophilic mechanism over a radical process, as no viable single‐electron donor is available to generate a sulfonyl radical under the reaction conditions. The reaction likely proceeds via electrophilic addition of PhSO2+ to protonated coumarin, followed by a thermodynamically favorable deprotonation step. Overall, this Brønsted acid–assisted sulfonylation provides an efficient, regioselective, and mechanistically well‐supported approach for the functionalization of coumarin and related heteroaromatic systems, thereby expanding the synthetic utility of direct CH sulfonylation reactions.
item.content:encoded: A Cu(OTf)2-mediated protocol for the regioselective CH sulfonylation of coumarin derivatives with arylsulfonyl chlorides is reported. This transformation exhibits broad substrate scope and excellent functional-group tolerance, affording a wide range of C6-sulfonylated coumarins in moderate to excellent yields. The methodology also extends to heteroaromatic frameworks, such as xanthone, and to bioactive scaffolds exemplified by methoxsalane, enabling selective CH sulfonylation even at unconventional positions. Mechanistic investigations, supported by DFT calculations and Fukui function analysis, reveal that transient protonation of coumarin by in situ–generated triflic acid significantly enhances electrophilic susceptibility at the C6 position, thus overriding the intrinsic C3 reactivity of the neutral scaffold. Computational results favor an electrophilic mechanism over a radical process, as no viable single-electron donor is available to generate a sulfonyl radical under the reaction conditions. The reaction likely proceeds via electrophilic addition of PhSO2 + to protonated coumarin, followed by a thermodynamically favorable deprotonation step. Overall, this Brønsted acid–assisted sulfonylation provides an efficient, regioselective, and mechanistically well-supported approach for the functionalization of coumarin and related heteroaromatic systems, thereby expanding the synthetic utility of direct CH sulfonylation reactions.
item.dc:creator: Mukeshkumar Yadav, Pranali Prabhakar Thakur, Mahendra Patil
item.dc:title: Brønsted Acid–Driven C6‐Selective Sulfonylation of Coumarins Promoted by Cu(OTf)2: Synthesis of Coumarin–Sulfone Scaffolds

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Transfer Hydrogenation of Organic Azides to Amines by Ammonia Borane Complex Under Mild Conditions Catalyzed by Reusable Au Nanoparticles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Organic azides are readily reduced to amines under mild conditions by ammonia borane complex, catalyzed by a low loading level of reusable Au nanoparticles supported on TiO2.
We present the first protocol for the chemoselective reduction of organic azides to amines by ammonia borane complex. In the presence of a low catalytic amount of Au nanoparticles supported on TiO2 (0.5 mol%), alkyl and aryl azides are readily reduced to amines by NH3BH3 (1 M equivalent) within 15–30 min at room temperature in a protic solvent such as ethanol or methanol. The reduction of the azide functionality occurs faster relative to coexisting nitro group, while other formally reducible functionalities such as cyano, ester or halides remain intact. The catalyst can be easily recycled and reused equally well at least 3 consecutive times.
item.content:encoded:
Organic azides are readily reduced to amines under mild conditions by ammonia borane complex, catalyzed by a low loading level of reusable Au nanoparticles supported on TiO2.


We present the first protocol for the chemoselective reduction of organic azides to amines by ammonia borane complex. In the presence of a low catalytic amount of Au nanoparticles supported on TiO2 (0.5 mol%), alkyl and aryl azides are readily reduced to amines by NH3BH3 (1 M equivalent) within 15–30 min at room temperature in a protic solvent such as ethanol or methanol. The reduction of the azide functionality occurs faster relative to coexisting nitro group, while other formally reducible functionalities such as cyano, ester or halides remain intact. The catalyst can be easily recycled and reused equally well at least 3 consecutive times.
item.dc:creator: Elisavet‐Maria Zantioti‐Chatzouda, Dimitra Malliotaki, Ioannis Zachilas, Andreas‐Drakos Kokkinos, Orestis Agathocleous, Manolis Stratakis
item.dc:title: Transfer Hydrogenation of Organic Azides to Amines by Ammonia Borane Complex Under Mild Conditions Catalyzed by Reusable Au Nanoparticles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Iodine‐Catalyzed C(sp3)–H Amination for the Synthesis of 2‐Arylquinazolines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
An efficient electrochemical C(sp3)–H amination cyclization was developed for the synthesis of substituted quinazolines in the presence of an iodine catalyst. A series of 2‐arylquinazolines can be obtained with good yields from readily available starting materials by virtue of the new avenue. The reaction features excellent substrate applicability, metal‐ and chemical oxidant‐free conditions, and the principles of green chemistry.
An efficient electrochemical C(sp3)–H amination cyclization was developed under mild conditions in the presence of an iodine catalyst. A series of substituted quinazolines can be obtained with good yields by virtue of the new avenue. The reaction features excellent substrate applicability, scalability, metal‐free, and chemical oxidant‐free.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient electrochemical C(sp3)–H amination cyclization was developed for the synthesis of substituted quinazolines in the presence of an iodine catalyst. A series of 2-arylquinazolines can be obtained with good yields from readily available starting materials by virtue of the new avenue. The reaction features excellent substrate applicability, metal- and chemical oxidant-free conditions, and the principles of green chemistry.


An efficient electrochemical C(sp3)–H amination cyclization was developed under mild conditions in the presence of an iodine catalyst. A series of substituted quinazolines can be obtained with good yields by virtue of the new avenue. The reaction features excellent substrate applicability, scalability, metal-free, and chemical oxidant-free.
item.dc:creator: Yupu Zhang, Kai Li, Hao Yang, Qi Sun, Zhiyong Wang
item.dc:title: Electrochemical Iodine‐Catalyzed C(sp3)–H Amination for the Synthesis of 2‐Arylquinazolines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Facile Synthesis of Carbamates via Base‐Promoted There‐Component Coupling of Aziridines with CO2 and Amines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
A base‐promoted, metal‐free three‐component coupling reaction involving aziridines, amines, and CO2 has been developed for the first time. This method enables efficient and highly regioselective synthesis of a diverse range of valuable carbamates in high yields. The protocol benefits from mild reaction conditions, broad substrate scope, and excellent functional group tolerance.
Carbamates represent an important class of organic compounds with wide applications. Traditional methods for their synthesis often rely on the use of phosgene and its derivatives. Herein, a base‐promoted three‐component coupling reaction between aziridines, carbon dioxide (CO2), and amines has been successfully developed for the first time, providing an efficient route to a range of structurally diverse and valuable carbamates in high yields with excellent regioselectivities. The metal‐free reaction features mild reaction conditions, high functional group tolerance, and wide substrate scope.
item.content:encoded:
A base-promoted, metal-free three-component coupling reaction involving aziridines, amines, and CO2 has been developed for the first time. This method enables efficient and highly regioselective synthesis of a diverse range of valuable carbamates in high yields. The protocol benefits from mild reaction conditions, broad substrate scope, and excellent functional group tolerance.


Carbamates represent an important class of organic compounds with wide applications. Traditional methods for their synthesis often rely on the use of phosgene and its derivatives. Herein, a base-promoted three-component coupling reaction between aziridines, carbon dioxide (CO2), and amines has been successfully developed for the first time, providing an efficient route to a range of structurally diverse and valuable carbamates in high yields with excellent regioselectivities. The metal-free reaction features mild reaction conditions, high functional group tolerance, and wide substrate scope.
item.dc:creator: Ziyang Li, Li Wei, Zhongrong Xu, Yanwei Ren, Huanfeng Jiang, Chaorong Qi
item.dc:title: Facile Synthesis of Carbamates via Base‐Promoted There‐Component Coupling of Aziridines with CO2 and Amines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: EDA Complex–Mediated C(sp3)–H Cross‐Dehydrogenative Coupling Enables Synthesis of Noncanonical α,β‐Diamino Acids
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Access to nonproteinogenic α,β‐diamino acids from simple feedstock precursors is of great interest to generate medicinal small molecule libraries. We report a regioselective photoinduced C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross‐dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) of N‐arylglycine derivatives with amides. This reaction proceeds via an electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex of electron‐deficient aryl halides to generate reactive aryl radicals capable of efficient hydrogen‐atom transfer (HAT) processes.
Nonproteinogenic α,β‐diamino acids are key motifs in bioactive compounds. We report a photoinduced cross‐dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) of N‐arylglycine derivatives with amides. This reaction proceeds via an electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex that enables regioselective C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross‐coupling, providing access to non‐canonical α,β‐diamino acids from simple precursors.
item.content:encoded:
Access to nonproteinogenic α,β-diamino acids from simple feedstock precursors is of great interest to generate medicinal small molecule libraries. We report a regioselective photoinduced C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) of N-arylglycine derivatives with amides. This reaction proceeds via an electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex of electron-deficient aryl halides to generate reactive aryl radicals capable of efficient hydrogen-atom transfer (HAT) processes.


Nonproteinogenic α,β-diamino acids are key motifs in bioactive compounds. We report a photoinduced cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) of N-arylglycine derivatives with amides. This reaction proceeds via an electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex that enables regioselective C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross-coupling, providing access to non-canonical α,β-diamino acids from simple precursors.
item.dc:creator: Krishnakumar Sachidanandan, Cole Stenftenagel, Athul Joshy, Alexander M. Cluff, Sébastien Laulhé
item.dc:title: EDA Complex–Mediated C(sp3)–H Cross‐Dehydrogenative Coupling Enables Synthesis of Noncanonical α,β‐Diamino Acids

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Enzymatic Synthesis of Demethylated Sesquiterpenes From 14‐nor‐Farnesyl Pyrophosphate
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
The substrate analog 14‐nor‐FPP was synthesised and enzymatically converted with 10 bacterial and fungal sesquiterpene synthases, resulting in the isolation of 12 demethylated sesquiterpenes in yields up to 17%. In most cases the demethyl analogs of the FPP‐derived natural products were obtained, but also different products showing modified reactivities of the substrate analogs were formed.
Previous work has revealed that terpene synthases can efficiently convert non‐natural substrate analogs, which often leads to interesting new structures. In the present study 10 sesquiterpene synthases were used to convert 14‐nor‐FPP into demethyl (C14) sesquiterpenes. The obtained products were isolated and their structures elucidated through NMR spectroscopy. In most cases the demethyl analogs corresponding to the natural products formed from farnesyl pyrophosphate were obtained, but sometimes the structural change in the substrate resulted in a change of reactivity and consequently the formation of structurally deviating products. The absolute configurations were assigned based on biosynthetic considerations and on a comparison of optical rotations to those of the natural C15 analogs.
item.content:encoded:
The substrate analog 14-nor-FPP was synthesised and enzymatically converted with 10 bacterial and fungal sesquiterpene synthases, resulting in the isolation of 12 demethylated sesquiterpenes in yields up to 17%. In most cases the demethyl analogs of the FPP-derived natural products were obtained, but also different products showing modified reactivities of the substrate analogs were formed.


Previous work has revealed that terpene synthases can efficiently convert non-natural substrate analogs, which often leads to interesting new structures. In the present study 10 sesquiterpene synthases were used to convert 14-nor-FPP into demethyl (C14) sesquiterpenes. The obtained products were isolated and their structures elucidated through NMR spectroscopy. In most cases the demethyl analogs corresponding to the natural products formed from farnesyl pyrophosphate were obtained, but sometimes the structural change in the substrate resulted in a change of reactivity and consequently the formation of structurally deviating products. The absolute configurations were assigned based on biosynthetic considerations and on a comparison of optical rotations to those of the natural C15 analogs.
item.dc:creator: Kizerbo A. Taizoumbe, Jeroen S. Dickschat
item.dc:title: Enzymatic Synthesis of Demethylated Sesquiterpenes From 14‐nor‐Farnesyl Pyrophosphate

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Catalytic Hydrogenation of Lignin‐Derived Aromatics With Rhodium Oxide in Water
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
The easily available, easy‐to‐handle rhodium(III) oxide, Rh2O3, is shown to be a competent catalyst for arene hydrogenation, including chemoselective reduction of α,β‐unsaturated aryl ketones.
Catalytic arene hydrogenation offers a direct pathway to saturated carbocycles. It can also be exploited to upgrade lignin‐derived aromatics. We have found that one of the most easily available rhodium compounds, rhodium(III) oxide, (Rh2O3), is a highly efficient heterogeneous catalyst for arene hydrogenation in aqueous conditions. A diverse array of arene substrates, including those derived from lignin, underwent smooth conversion into their corresponding saturated products under 5 bar of hydrogen in water. This hydrogenation protocol displayed good compatibility with functional groups and showed tuneable chemoselectivity for α,β‐unsaturated aryl ketones. Given the easy commercial availability of Rh2O3, the method provides a convenient route for synthesising three‐dimensional alicyclic compounds from two‐dimensional aromatic precursors, including those readily accessible from lignin depolymerisation.
item.content:encoded:
The easily available, easy-to-handle rhodium(III) oxide, Rh2O3, is shown to be a competent catalyst for arene hydrogenation, including chemoselective reduction of α,β-unsaturated aryl ketones.


Catalytic arene hydrogenation offers a direct pathway to saturated carbocycles. It can also be exploited to upgrade lignin-derived aromatics. We have found that one of the most easily available rhodium compounds, rhodium(III) oxide, (Rh2O3), is a highly efficient heterogeneous catalyst for arene hydrogenation in aqueous conditions. A diverse array of arene substrates, including those derived from lignin, underwent smooth conversion into their corresponding saturated products under 5 bar of hydrogen in water. This hydrogenation protocol displayed good compatibility with functional groups and showed tuneable chemoselectivity for α,β-unsaturated aryl ketones. Given the easy commercial availability of Rh2O3, the method provides a convenient route for synthesising three-dimensional alicyclic compounds from two-dimensional aromatic precursors, including those readily accessible from lignin depolymerisation.
item.dc:creator: Zhenyu Chen, Leiming Qi, Sydney Williams, Ilkan Calisir, Shamsal Haq, Rasmita Raval, Changzhi Li, Tao Zhang, Jianliang Xiao
item.dc:title: Catalytic Hydrogenation of Lignin‐Derived Aromatics With Rhodium Oxide in Water

item.title: Front Cover: Light Driven Acid and Base Controlled Selective Synthesis of 3,4‐Dihydroquinoxalinones and Quinoxalinones via Cascade Cyclization of Aryl Diazoacetates With o‐Phenylenediamines (Eur. J. Org. Chem. 14/2026)
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description: The Front Cover shows the acid–base‐controlled selective synthesis of 3,4‐dihydroquinoxalinones and quinoxalinones through photoredox cascade cyclization of aryl diazoacetates with o‐phenylenediamines. The red and blue colors symbolize the reaction pathway environments under acidic and basic conditions, respectively, and the ambient molecular oxygen represents an environmentally benign oxidant for the synthesis of quinoxalinone derivatives. More information can be found in the Research Article by R. Anandhan and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/ejoc.202500963).

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Undirected Primary C─H Functionalization of Alkanes via Hydrogen Atom Transfer
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Undirected primary CH functionalization has recently been realized through hydrogen atom transfer. These studies are highlighted in this review, with particular emphasis on the primary selectivity of different kinds of substrates as well as the origin of primary selectivity.
Hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) which can produce radical intermediates from alkanes provides a strategy to achieve undirected C(sp3)‐H functionalization. Typically, primary CH bonds are the least reactive in HAT processes, compared to tertiary CH or activated benzylic CH. It is noteworthy that HAT‐mediated primary CH functionalization has recently been realized through the adjustment of various factors such as the catalyst or reagent design. This review summarizes recent advances in undirected primary CH functionalization via the HAT process. These examples will be discussed according to the HAT reagent. Reaction conditions, primary selectivity of various substrates, mechanism, and origin of the primary selectivity will be highlighted.
item.content:encoded:
Undirected primary CH functionalization has recently been realized through hydrogen atom transfer. These studies are highlighted in this review, with particular emphasis on the primary selectivity of different kinds of substrates as well as the origin of primary selectivity.

Typically, primary CH bonds are the least reactive in HAT processes, compared to tertiary CH or activated benzylic CH. It is noteworthy that HAT-mediated primary CH functionalization has recently been realized through the adjustment of various factors such as the catalyst or reagent design. This review summarizes recent advances in undirected primary CH functionalization via the HAT process. These examples will be discussed according to the HAT reagent. Reaction conditions, primary selectivity of various substrates, mechanism, and origin of the primary selectivity will be highlighted.

item.dc:title: Undirected Primary C─H Functionalization of Alkanes via Hydrogen Atom Transfer

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Approaches Toward Arylation Reactions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
This review provides a systematic analysis of electrochemical arylation reactions. Various reactions such as C‐H arylation, α‐arylation, oxidative arylation, reductive arylation, decarboxylative arylation, O‐arylation, and other miscellaneous transformations has been discussed with their mechanistic insights.
Electrochemical arylation has emerged as a sustainable and efficient approach for forming CC and Cheteroatom bonds. These reactions provide a green alternative to conventional arylation methods that often rely on harsh reaction conditions. The present review categorizes electrochemical arylation reactions into key types, such as C–H arylation, α‐arylation, oxidative arylation, reductive arylation, decarboxylative arylation, O‐arylation, and other miscellaneous transformations. Each class highlights distinct mechanistic features and substrate scopes, demonstrating the versatility of electrochemical techniques in constructing diverse arylated frameworks. Overall, this review provides a comprehensive understanding of how electrochemical strategies are revolutionizing arylation chemistry and paving the way toward more sustainable organic synthesis.
item.content:encoded:

Various reactions such as C-H arylation, α-arylation, oxidative arylation, reductive arylation, decarboxylative arylation, O-arylation, and other miscellaneous transformations has been discussed with their mechanistic insights.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Sulfur Heterocycles by Palladium‐Catalyzed Cyclization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
In spite of the well‐known palladium thiophilicity, sulfur‐containing acyclic substrates may efficiently undergo several different kinds of palladium‐catalyzed cyclization processes leading to highly important sulfur heterocycles in one synthetic step. Advances in this challenging and stimulating field of research are presented in this review.
The minireview is aimed at giving an overview on palladium‐catalyzed cyclization methods for the synthesis of important sulfur heterocycles, including thiophenes, benzothiophenes, benzothiazoles, and sulfur‐containing polycyclic systems. The minireview is organized into several sections based on the kind of reactivity leading to the sulfur heterocycle, namely: Pd‐catalyzed cyclization of aryl and vinyl halide derivatives; Pd‐catalyzed cyclization by C—H activation; Pd‐catalyzed cyclization of olefinic substrates; Pd‐catalyzed cyclization of acetylenic substrates; Pd‐catalyzed carbonylative cyclization; cyclization by Pd‐catalyzed ring opening—ring closure. The review covers Pd‐catalyzed S‐cyclizations as well as transformations in which the sulfur atom lies within the chain undergoing annulation.
item.content:encoded:

Advances in this challenging and stimulating field of research are presented in this review.


The minireview is aimed at giving an overview on palladium-catalyzed cyclization methods for the synthesis of important sulfur heterocycles, including thiophenes, benzothiophenes, benzothiazoles, and sulfur-containing polycyclic systems. The minireview is organized into several sections based on the kind of reactivity leading to the sulfur heterocycle, namely: Pd-catalyzed cyclization of aryl and vinyl halide derivatives; Pd-catalyzed cyclization by C—H activation; Pd-catalyzed cyclization of olefinic substrates; Pd-catalyzed cyclization of acetylenic substrates; Pd-catalyzed carbonylative cyclization; cyclization by Pd-catalyzed ring opening—ring closure. The review covers Pd-catalyzed S-cyclizations as well as transformations in which the sulfur atom lies within the chain undergoing annulation.

item.dc:title: Synthesis of Sulfur Heterocycles by Palladium‐Catalyzed Cyclization

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Progress in Transition‐Metal‐Catalyzed C–H Functionalization of 2‐Arylthiazoles and 2‐Arylbenzothiazoles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 14, 15 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in transition‐metal‐catalyzed regioselective C–H functionalization of 2‐arylthiazoles and 2‐arylbenzothiazoles, covering a broad spectrum of C–C and C–X bond‐forming transformations. Key catalytic strategies, mechanistic insights, and emerging methodologies are highlighted, providing guidance for the selective diversification of sulfur‐containing heteroarenes in medicinal and materials chemistry.
Heterocyclic compounds play a central role in modern organic chemistry, underpinning the structural frameworks of numerous bioactive molecules and functional materials. In particular, 2‐arylthiazoles and 2‐arylbenzothiazoles have emerged as privileged sulfur‐containing heteroarenes, valued for their diverse biological activities and favorable electronic properties. Their rigid, π‐conjugated architectures enable broad applications ranging from medicinal chemistry to optoelectronic materials. Consequently, efficient methods for their synthesis and selective structural modification are of considerable importance. While classical synthetic routes and cross‐coupling strategies provide access to these scaffolds, their reliance on prefunctionalized substrates and multistep sequences limits synthetic efficiency and late‐stage diversification. In response, transition‐metal‐catalyzed C–H functionalization has become a powerful alternative, allowing direct and atom‐economical modification of heteroarenes. Recent advances have addressed the inherent challenges of sulfur‐containing substrates, enabling precise regioselective and regiodivergent transformations. This review summarizes recent progress in transition‐metal‐catalyzed C–H functionalization of 2‐arylthiazoles and 2‐arylbenzothiazoles, highlighting key reaction modes, mechanistic insights, and representative applications.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent advances in transition-metal-catalyzed regioselective C–H functionalization of 2-arylthiazoles and 2-arylbenzothiazoles, covering a broad spectrum of C–C and C–X bond-forming transformations. Key catalytic strategies, mechanistic insights, and emerging methodologies are highlighted, providing guidance for the selective diversification of sulfur-containing heteroarenes in medicinal and materials chemistry.


Heterocyclic compounds play a central role in modern organic chemistry, underpinning the structural frameworks of numerous bioactive molecules and functional materials. In response, transition-metal-catalyzed C–H functionalization has become a powerful alternative, allowing direct and atom-economical modification of heteroarenes. item.dc:title: Progress in Transition‐Metal‐Catalyzed C–H Functionalization of 2‐Arylthiazoles and 2‐Arylbenzothiazoles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Zwitterionic C–N Bond Cleavage by the Accumulation of Charge: Green Synthesis of Amino Thiazolyl Benzimidazolium Bromide and Benzimidazol‐Thiazole under Microwave Irradiations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The reaction was carried out for the C–N bond cleavage by the accumulation of charge. The zwitterionic intermediate stabilized by cyclic hydrogen bonding. The accumulation of positive charge at lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and accumulation of negative charge at highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) decreases the HOMO‐LUMO band gap and resulted to C–N bond cleavage. L‐proline was used as organocatalyst under microwave irradiations.
The Zwitterionic curious methodology for the regioselective C–N bond cleavage to synthesize the anticancer substituted amino thiazolyl benzimidazolium bromide and its thiazole. The cyclic hydrogen bonding (multiple hydrogen bonding) approach with accumulation of charge have been applied for the regioselective C–N bond cleavage by organocatalysis. It is metal free one‐pot reaction fascinated by microwave irradiations. The zwitterionic L‐proline have been proposed as catalyst and acceptor partner in donor–acceptor cyclic hydrogen bonding system. The work is green and sustainable without separation, purification and solvent extraction. The respective accumulation of positive charge and negative charge at lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) decreases energy gap in cyclic hydrogen bonding. The accumulation of positive charge on two carbons executed the C–N bond cleavage effectively by the migration of benzimidazole moiety. The density functional theory is used to study the intermediates using HOMO‐LUMO frontier orbital concept. This elite cyclic hydrogen bonding between bromide salt and neutral thiazole moiety abetted the solidification of product in solution. The synthesized products are anticancer agents. All products created new library of amino thiazolyl benzimidazolium bromide and their thiazole heterocycles.

L-proline was used as organocatalyst under microwave irradiations.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: 1,2‐Diazetidines − Structure, Synthesis, and Functionalization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
1,2‐Diazetidines are saturated four‐membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite recent advances in their synthesis and promising potential in medicinal chemistry, the chemistry of these cyclic hydrazines remains underexplored. This review highlights their three‐dimensional structure and explores various strategies for their preparation and functionalization.
1,2‐Diazetidines are saturated four‐membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite their promising potential as rigid molecular scaffolds for drug discovery, their synthesis and reactivity remain largely unexplored. This review summarizes their three‐dimensional structural features, which have been characterized using various spectroscopic and crystallographic methods. Current synthetic approaches of 1,2‐diazetidines fall into three main categories: (i) [2 + 2] cycloadditions between azo compounds and electron‐rich olefins, (ii) photochemical ring contraction of dihydropyridazines, and (iii) intramolecular ring closure of hydrazine derivatives, the latter enabling stereocontrol at the four‐membered ring. Finally, the reactivity and functionalization of these cyclic hydrazines are discussed, including derivatization at both nitrogen atoms.
item.content:encoded:
1,2-Diazetidines are saturated four-membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite recent advances in their synthesis and promising potential in medicinal chemistry, the chemistry of these cyclic hydrazines remains underexplored. This review highlights their three-dimensional structure and explores various strategies for their preparation and functionalization.


1,2-Diazetidines are saturated four-membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite their promising potential as rigid molecular scaffolds for drug discovery, their synthesis and reactivity remain largely unexplored. This review summarizes their three-dimensional structural features, which have been characterized using various spectroscopic and crystallographic methods. Current synthetic approaches of 1,2-diazetidines fall into three main categories: (i) [2 + 2] cycloadditions between azo compounds and electron-rich olefins, (ii) photochemical ring contraction of dihydropyridazines, and (iii) intramolecular ring closure of hydrazine derivatives, the latter enabling stereocontrol at the four-membered ring. Finally, the reactivity and functionalization of these cyclic hydrazines are discussed, including derivatization at both nitrogen atoms.
item.dc:creator: Stefan Roesner
item.dc:title: 1,2‐Diazetidines − Structure, Synthesis, and Functionalization

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Maleimides Revisited: Reactivity, Selectivity, and Emerging Synthetic Strategies
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Maleimides are presented as electronically tunable α,β‐unsaturated platforms whose reactivity extends far beyond classical Michael chemistry. Recent advances in catalytic C–H activation, conjugate addition, and cycloaddition strategies enable precise control of regioselectivity, stereoselectivity, and π‐system retention. Emerging hybrid approaches expand their role as adaptable building blocks for complex molecule construction and translational applications.
Maleimides are α,β‐unsaturated cyclic imides that serve as electronically tunable α,β‐unsaturated scaffolds enabling controlled bond construction. Recent advances have redefined maleimides from simple Michael acceptors into programmable electrophilic platforms that enable controlled regio‐, stereo‐, and chemoselective bond formation. Over the past 5 years, this versatile scaffold has seen renewed attention in forming CC, CN, and CS bonds through transition‐metal‐catalyzed alkylation, alkenylation, organocatalytic and metal‐catalyzed conjugate additions, and diverse cycloaddition reactions. This review summarizes recent synthetic approaches to maleimide construction and functionalization, with particular focus on nucleophilic additions involving carbon and heteroatom pronucleophiles, enantioselective metal‐catalyzed transformations including C–H activation, and [4 + 2], [3 + 2], and [2 + 2] cycloaddition strategies leading to structurally complex frameworks. Emphasis is placed on methodologies that address long‐standing challenges in maleimide chemistry, including control of selectivity, retention of unsaturation, and expansion of post‐functionalization through emerging and hybrid catalytic strategies.
item.content:encoded:

Recent advances in catalytic C–H activation, conjugate addition, and cycloaddition strategies enable precise control of regioselectivity, stereoselectivity, and π-system retention. Emerging hybrid approaches expand their role as adaptable building blocks for complex molecule construction and translational applications.

Recent advances have redefined maleimides from simple Michael acceptors into programmable electrophilic platforms that enable controlled regio-, stereo-, and chemoselective bond formation. Over the past 5 years, this versatile scaffold has seen renewed attention in forming CC, CN, and CS bonds through transition-metal-catalyzed alkylation, alkenylation, organocatalytic and metal-catalyzed conjugate additions, and diverse cycloaddition reactions. This review summarizes recent synthetic approaches to maleimide construction and functionalization, with particular focus on nucleophilic additions involving carbon and heteroatom pronucleophiles, enantioselective metal-catalyzed transformations including C–H activation, and [4 + 2], [3 + 2], and [2 + 2] cycloaddition strategies leading to structurally complex frameworks. Emphasis is placed on methodologies that address long-standing challenges in maleimide chemistry, including control of selectivity, retention of unsaturation, and expansion of post-functionalization through emerging and hybrid catalytic strategies.
item.dc:creator: Gizele Celante, Katiana Simões Kittelson, Raquel Possemozer Santos, Allana C. F. Martins, Roberto S. Gomes
item.dc:title: Maleimides Revisited: Reactivity, Selectivity, and Emerging Synthetic Strategies

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Catalyzed Ring‐Opening Halogenation of Spiro‐Dihydroquinazolinones Using Perhalogenated Methanes as Halogen Sources
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A cascade reaction involving spiro C—C σ–bond cleavage and C–halogen bond formation is reported for the efficient construction of bromo‐/chloroalkyl‐substituted quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones from spiro‐dihydroquinazolinones and perhalogenated methanes under organophotoredox catalysis.
Visible‐light‐catalyzed aromatization‐driven deconstructive bromination and chlorination of spiro‐dihydroquinazolinones are described, with perhalogenated methanes serving as halogenating agents. A series of remotely and site‐specifically bromoalkyl‐ and chloroalkyl‐substituted quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones were obtained with moderate yields and functional‐group tolerance. A radical pathway involving nitrogen‐centered‐radical‐induced C─C bond cleavage and carbon–halogen bond formation is proposed for this transformation.
item.content:encoded:
A cascade reaction involving spiro C—C σ–bond cleavage and C–halogen bond formation is reported for the efficient construction of bromo-/chloroalkyl-substituted quinazolin-4(3H)-ones from spiro-dihydroquinazolinones and perhalogenated methanes under organophotoredox catalysis.


Visible-light-catalyzed aromatization-driven deconstructive bromination and chlorination of spiro-dihydroquinazolinones are described, with perhalogenated methanes serving as halogenating agents. A series of remotely and site-specifically bromoalkyl- and chloroalkyl-substituted quinazolin-4(3H)-ones were obtained with moderate yields and functional-group tolerance. A radical pathway involving nitrogen-centered-radical-induced C─C bond cleavage and carbon–halogen bond formation is proposed for this transformation.
item.dc:creator: Yan Hao, Tao Wang, Jin-Yang Zhan, Jun-Ru Li, Shenghu Yan, Meng-Fan Li, Jia-Yin Wang
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Catalyzed Ring‐Opening Halogenation of Spiro‐Dihydroquinazolinones Using Perhalogenated Methanes as Halogen Sources

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Homopropargyl Alcohols via Zinc Iodide Mediated Three‐Component Reaction of Propargyl Carbamates With Organoboranes and Aldehydes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This study presents a novel and practical method for synthesizing homopropargyl alcohols via a zinc iodide‐mediated three‐component reaction of terminal alkyne‐bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes under mild reaction conditions. A wide range of α,γ‐substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2‐metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom.
Homopropargyl alcohols are versatile building blocks in a wide range of synthetic transformations; consequently, the development of efficient and practical methods for their preparation remains an important objective in organic synthesis. This study presents a novel and practical zinc iodide mediated three‐component reaction of terminal alkyne‐bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes for the synthesis of homopropargyl alcohols. A variety of α,γ‐substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2‐metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom, which subsequently react with aldehydes to afford the corresponding homopropargyl alcohols. In contrast to conventional methods for the generation of alkynylborates, the present system requires only a catalytic amount of lithium diisopropylamide as the base.
item.content:encoded:
This study presents a novel and practical method for synthesizing homopropargyl alcohols via a zinc iodide-mediated three-component reaction of terminal alkyne-bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes under mild reaction conditions. A wide range of α,γ-substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2-metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom.


Homopropargyl alcohols are versatile building blocks in a wide range of synthetic transformations; consequently, the development of efficient and practical methods for their preparation remains an important objective in organic synthesis. This study presents a novel and practical zinc iodide mediated three-component reaction of terminal alkyne-bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes for the synthesis of homopropargyl alcohols. A variety of α,γ-substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2-metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom, which subsequently react with aldehydes to afford the corresponding homopropargyl alcohols. In contrast to conventional methods for the generation of alkynylborates, the present system requires only a catalytic amount of lithium diisopropylamide as the base.
item.dc:creator: Nene Murata, Issa Honma, Yoshikazu Horino
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Homopropargyl Alcohols via Zinc Iodide Mediated Three‐Component Reaction of Propargyl Carbamates With Organoboranes and Aldehydes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Regioselective Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Hydroamination of Ynamides With Phenyl Isocyanates
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)‐1‐(1‐(2‐oxooxazolidin‐3‐yl)‐2‐phenylvinyl)‐1,3‐diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)‐catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed.
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)‐1‐(1‐(2‐oxooxazolidin‐3‐yl)‐2‐phenylvinyl)−1,3‐diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)‐catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed. This protocol features excellent regioselectivity with a wide range of substrate scopes of phenyl isocyanates and ynamides. Using this method, highly substituted oxooxazolidinyl phenylvinyl 1,3‐diphenylurea compounds were synthesized in moderate to excellent yields.
item.content:encoded:
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)-1-(1-(2-oxooxazolidin-3-yl)-2-phenylvinyl)-1,3-diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)-catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed.


A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)-1-(1-(2-oxooxazolidin-3-yl)-2-phenylvinyl)−1,3-diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)-catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed. This protocol features excellent regioselectivity with a wide range of substrate scopes of phenyl isocyanates and ynamides. Using this method, highly substituted oxooxazolidinyl phenylvinyl 1,3-diphenylurea compounds were synthesized in moderate to excellent yields.
item.dc:creator: Shubham Jaiswal, Amrendra Kumar, Narender Tadigoppula
item.dc:title: Highly Regioselective Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Hydroamination of Ynamides With Phenyl Isocyanates

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Silver‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Radical Aminofluorination of Arylalkenes with Selectfluor and Amino N‐Heterocycles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A three‐component aminofluorination fuses arylalkenes with Selectfluor and free amino N‐heterocycles, delivering diverse β‐fluoro heterocyclic amines in up to 98% yield.
A silver‐catalyzed three‐component radical aminofluorination of arylalkenes has been developed, utilizing free N‐heterocyclic amines and Selectfluor as key reagents. Under mild conditions, a diverse array of β‐fluoroamine derivatives were obtained in moderate to excellent yields (up to 98%). The protocol exhibits broad substrate scope, tolerating various functional groups such as halogens, alkyls, esters, and cyano groups on both the amino heterocycle and alkene components. Mechanistic studies support a radical pathway involving silver‐mediated oxidation of the amino heterocycle to generate an amino radical, followed by addition to the alkene and subsequent fluorine atom transfer. This method provides a straightforward route to valuable monofluoroalkylated amino N‐heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:
A three-component aminofluorination fuses arylalkenes with Selectfluor and free amino N-heterocycles, delivering diverse β-fluoro heterocyclic amines in up to 98% yield.


A silver-catalyzed three-component radical aminofluorination of arylalkenes has been developed, utilizing free N-heterocyclic amines and Selectfluor as key reagents. Under mild conditions, a diverse array of β-fluoroamine derivatives were obtained in moderate to excellent yields (up to 98%). The protocol exhibits broad substrate scope, tolerating various functional groups such as halogens, alkyls, esters, and cyano groups on both the amino heterocycle and alkene components. Mechanistic studies support a radical pathway involving silver-mediated oxidation of the amino heterocycle to generate an amino radical, followed by addition to the alkene and subsequent fluorine atom transfer. This method provides a straightforward route to valuable monofluoroalkylated amino N-heterocycles.
item.dc:creator: Qiangqiang Liu, Xiao‐Lin Wang, Xiao‐Ming Zhao, Wenyan Dan, Sheng‐Cai Zheng
item.dc:title: Silver‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Radical Aminofluorination of Arylalkenes with Selectfluor and Amino N‐Heterocycles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Push–Pull Ynamines and Push–Pull Ynamides: Synthesis, Structure, Reactivity, and Application
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Push–pull ynamines and push–pull ynamides, which carry an EWG and an amino or amido group as EDG, are highly interesting classes of EDG‐EWG alkynes that have been intensively studied due to their exceptional reactivity. In this review, the synthesis, structure, reactivity, and application of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynecarbamates, push–pull ynesulfonamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynesulfonamides, and push–pull enynamines are comprehensively discussed.
Push–pull ynamines, whose triple bond carries a disubstituted amino group as electron‐donating group (EDG) and an electron‐withdrawing group (EWG), are characterized by a π‐delocalization (push–pull effect) and a polarized triple bond. Push–pull ynamines exhibit the reactivity of both ynamines and EWG alkynes together with reactivities not exhibited by either class of alkyne. Their structure, in particular the push–pull effect, and high reactivity toward electrophiles, nucleophiles, and cycloaddition have been intensively studied. Push–pull oligoynamines, which carry two to four triple bonds and push–pull enynamines, which contain a double and a triple bond, are further push–pull ynamines whose synthesis, structure, and reactivity have attracted considerable interest. A second class of unique EDG‐EWG substituted alkynes are push–pull ynamides, whose triple bond holds an amido group as EDG and an EWG. Push–pull ynamides are more thermally stable but significantly less reactive than push–pull ynamines. The push–pull effect and the polarization of the triple bond are probably less pronounced in push–pull ynamides than in push–pull ynamines. Push–pull ynesulfonamides and push–pull ynecarbamates have been primarily studied in transition metal‐catalyzed reactions and cycloaddition. Keteniminium ions are key intermediates in the reaction of push–pull ynamines and push–pull ynamides with electrophiles. This review summarizes the chemistry of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynamides, and push–pull enynamines, focusing on their synthesis, structure, reactivity, and synthetic application.
item.content:encoded:
Push–pull ynamines and push–pull ynamides, which carry an EWG and an amino or amido group as EDG, are highly interesting classes of EDG-EWG alkynes that have been intensively studied due to their exceptional reactivity. In this review, the synthesis, structure, reactivity, and application of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynecarbamates, push–pull ynesulfonamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynesulfonamides, and push–pull enynamines are comprehensively discussed.


Push–pull ynamines, whose triple bond carries a disubstituted amino group as electron-donating group (EDG) and an electron-withdrawing group (EWG), are characterized by a π-delocalization (push–pull effect) and a polarized triple bond. Push–pull ynamines exhibit the reactivity of both ynamines and EWG alkynes together with reactivities not exhibited by either class of alkyne. Their structure, in particular the push–pull effect, and high reactivity toward electrophiles, nucleophiles, and cycloaddition have been intensively studied. Push–pull oligoynamines, which carry two to four triple bonds and push–pull enynamines, which contain a double and a triple bond, are further push–pull ynamines whose synthesis, structure, and reactivity have attracted considerable interest. A second class of unique EDG-EWG substituted alkynes are push–pull ynamides, whose triple bond holds an amido group as EDG and an EWG. Push–pull ynamides are more thermally stable but significantly less reactive than push–pull ynamines. The push–pull effect and the polarization of the triple bond are probably less pronounced in push–pull ynamides than in push–pull ynamines. Push–pull ynesulfonamides and push–pull ynecarbamates have been primarily studied in transition metal-catalyzed reactions and cycloaddition. Keteniminium ions are key intermediates in the reaction of push–pull ynamines and push–pull ynamides with electrophiles. This review summarizes the chemistry of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynamides, and push–pull enynamines, focusing on their synthesis, structure, reactivity, and synthetic application.

item.dc:title: Push–Pull Ynamines and Push–Pull Ynamides: Synthesis, Structure, Reactivity, and Application

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Hydrogen Peroxide‐Initiated Polarity‐Inversion SNAr of Halophenols with Carboxylic Acids
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This report describes a hydrogen peroxide‐initiated SNAr reaction of halophenols with carboxylic acids using aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. The method converts halophenols into para‐acyloxy arenes under straightforward conditions, accommodates diverse carboxylic acids, and scales to gram quantities. Electrophile scope depends on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH, offering a practical carboxylate coupling strategy.
Polarity‐inversion SNAr offers a useful solution to the electronic limitations of classical two‐electron nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Here, we report a hydrogen peroxide‐initiated SNAr of halophenols with carboxylic acids using commercially available 30% aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. Under operationally straightforward conditions, this method converts readily available halophenols into para‐acyloxy arenes, accommodates a broad range of carboxylic acids, and is readily scalable to gram scale. The electrophile scope, however, shows a clear dependence on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH group. Overall, the present method provides a practical and complementary H2O2‐initiated option for thermally driven carboxylate coupling of halophenols.
item.content:encoded:
This report describes a hydrogen peroxide-initiated S N Ar reaction of halophenols with carboxylic acids using aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. The method converts halophenols into para-acyloxy arenes under straightforward conditions, accommodates diverse carboxylic acids, and scales to gram quantities. Electrophile scope depends on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH, offering a practical carboxylate coupling strategy.


Polarity-inversion S N Ar offers a useful solution to the electronic limitations of classical two-electron nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Here, we report a hydrogen peroxide-initiated S N Ar of halophenols with carboxylic acids using commercially available 30% aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. Under operationally straightforward conditions, this method converts readily available halophenols into para-acyloxy arenes, accommodates a broad range of carboxylic acids, and is readily scalable to gram scale. The electrophile scope, however, shows a clear dependence on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH group. Overall, the present method provides a practical and complementary H2O2-initiated option for thermally driven carboxylate coupling of halophenols.
item.dc:creator: Hui Zhao, Haofeng Shi, Lingzhi Xu, Jialiang Wu, Shuoshuo Zhang, Yunfei Du
item.dc:title: Hydrogen Peroxide‐Initiated Polarity‐Inversion SNAr of Halophenols with Carboxylic Acids

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Tri‐ and Tetrasubstituted Allyl Amines: Synthetic Strategies and Developments
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Polysubstituted allyl amines are among the important scaffolds in many natural products and biorelevant molecules. Moreover, these scaffolds are known for better pharmacological properties, metabolic stability, enhanced selectivity, and membrane permeability compared to simple allyl amines. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks confer distinctive steric and electronic characteristics, but they also pose significant synthetic challenges, such as steric congestion and difficulties in achieving precise control over regio‐ and E/Z‐selectivity. Hence, strategies for the synthesis of highly substituted counterparts remain limited. This review summarizes recent progress in the synthesis of tri‐ and tetrasubstituted allylamines, with particular emphasis on advances in transition‐metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement processes, and cross‐coupling strategies.
Highly substituted allyl amines, particularly tri‐ and tetrasubstituted variants, represent an important yet relatively underexplored class of nitrogen‐containing compounds with broad relevance in pharmaceuticals, natural product synthesis, and functional materials. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks impart unique steric and electronic features but also create substantial synthetic challenges, including steric congestion and difficulties in controlling regio‐ and E/Z‐selectivity. While mono‐ and disubstituted allyl amines are readily accessible through classical allylation and hydrofunctionalization methods, efficient and general strategies for highly substituted analogs remain limited. This review highlights recent advances in the synthesis of tri‐ and tetrasubstituted allyl amines, focusing on key developments in transition‐metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement, and cross‐coupling strategies.
item.content:encoded:
