item.dc:title: Total Synthesis of Corynanthe Alkaloids: Corynantheol, Isocorynantheol, and Dihydrocorynantheol

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemically Driven Domino Nucleophilic Addition/Diels–Alder Reaction: From 2‐Aminophenol Derivatives to Bridged Tricyclic Systems
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This electrochemical methodology allows the preparation of bridged tricyclic scaffolds through a rapid increase in the molecular complexity of simple and readily accessible starting materials. Alcohols proved to be the most efficient nucleophiles, as well as 6‐chloropurine. Plausibly, the process involves an anodic oxidative dearomatization to generate the key o‐quinone‐type intermediate.
The electrochemical methodology here reported allows the preparation of bridged tricyclic scaffolds through a rapid increase in the molecular complexity of simple and readily accessible starting materials. Standard conditions employing graphite as anode, nickel foam as cathode, and triflic acid as additive provided the target aza‐caged structures in good yields. Alcohols proved to be the most efficient nucleophiles, affording the desired products in modest yields, while nitrogen‐based nucleophiles generally resulted in decomposition, with the notable exception of 6‐chloropurine, which gave the desired product in moderate yield. Plausibly, the process involves an anodic oxidative dearomatization that generates an o‐quinone‐type intermediate, which undergoes nucleophilic addition followed by an intramolecular Diels–Alder reaction. Cyclic voltammetry confirmed the aminophenol as the electroactive species, supporting the proposed mechanistic pathway.
item.content:encoded:
This electrochemical methodology allows the preparation of bridged tricyclic scaffolds through a rapid increase in the molecular complexity of simple and readily accessible starting materials. Alcohols proved to be the most efficient nucleophiles, as well as 6-chloropurine. Plausibly, the process involves an anodic oxidative dearomatization to generate the key o-quinone-type intermediate.


The electrochemical methodology here reported allows the preparation of bridged tricyclic scaffolds through a rapid increase in the molecular complexity of simple and readily accessible starting materials. Standard conditions employing graphite as anode, nickel foam as cathode, and triflic acid as additive provided the target aza-caged structures in good yields. Alcohols proved to be the most efficient nucleophiles, affording the desired products in modest yields, while nitrogen-based nucleophiles generally resulted in decomposition, with the notable exception of 6-chloropurine, which gave the desired product in moderate yield. Plausibly, the process involves an anodic oxidative dearomatization that generates an o-quinone-type intermediate, which undergoes nucleophilic addition followed by an intramolecular Diels–Alder reaction. Cyclic voltammetry confirmed the aminophenol as the electroactive species, supporting the proposed mechanistic pathway.
item.dc:creator: Emanuele Cartamina, Sara Colombo, Davide Spanu, Gianluigi Broggini, Camilla Loro
item.dc:title: Electrochemically Driven Domino Nucleophilic Addition/Diels–Alder Reaction: From 2‐Aminophenol Derivatives to Bridged Tricyclic Systems

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Samarium(II) Ate Species Combined With Aminoalcohols as Reductants for Hydrogenation of Unsaturated Organic Compounds
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Samarium(II) ate species generated from SmI2, 3 equivalents of nBu4NCl, and a slight excess of L1H2 served as a superior reductant for reduction of 1‐substituted naphthalene derivatives, whose reactivity was higher than that with 2 equivalents of nBu4NCl or without nBu4NCl.
We explored the samarium(II)‐mediated reduction of unsaturated organic compounds using aminoalcohols as protic ligands. By screening various anionic (pseudo)halide ligands and their stoichiometry at the samarium(II) center, we found that samarium(II) ate species coordinated by chloride ligands exhibited superior reactivity. Furthermore, hydroxyethyl‐substituted ethylenediamine proved to be an effective supporting ligand as well as a proton source for the reduction of unsaturated organic compounds. UV–vis analyses of the anion exchange between SmI2 and halide sources (2 vs. 3 equiv.) revealed distinct absorption spectra depending on the amount of chloride, whereas bromide showed no significant spectral change.
item.content:encoded:
Samarium(II) ate species generated from SmI2, 3 equivalents of nBu4NCl, and a slight excess of L 1 H2 served as a superior reductant for reduction of 1-substituted naphthalene derivatives, whose reactivity was higher than that with 2 equivalents of nBu4NCl or without nBu4NCl.


We explored the samarium(II)-mediated reduction of unsaturated organic compounds using aminoalcohols as protic ligands. By screening various anionic (pseudo)halide ligands and their stoichiometry at the samarium(II) center, we found that samarium(II) ate species coordinated by chloride ligands exhibited superior reactivity. Furthermore, hydroxyethyl-substituted ethylenediamine proved to be an effective supporting ligand as well as a proton source for the reduction of unsaturated organic compounds. UV–vis analyses of the anion exchange between SmI2 and halide sources (2 vs. 3 equiv.) revealed distinct absorption spectra depending on the amount of chloride, whereas bromide showed no significant spectral change.
item.dc:creator: Yusuke Oda, Kulsum Bano, Hayato Tsurugi
item.dc:title: Samarium(II) Ate Species Combined With Aminoalcohols as Reductants for Hydrogenation of Unsaturated Organic Compounds

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Total Synthesis of Novel Oxylipins: Plasmodiophorols A, C, and Epimers
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The first total syntheses of plasmodiophorols A and C, recently identified α‐linolenic acid‐derived oxylipins, were achieved from chiral cyclopentafuranone in 12–13 steps. The routes use enzymatic desymmetrization, epimerization, and diastereoselective Grignard addition, providing stereocontrolled access to the natural products and reliable standards for biosynthetic and biological studies.
In 2021, novel oxylipins plasmodiophorol A–C were identified as products of the hydroperoxide bicyclase activity of CYP50918A1 from Plasmodiophora brassicae (Rhizaria, SAR). Here, we report the first total syntheses of plasmodiophorol A and C in 12 and 13 steps, respectively, starting from chiral tetrahydro‐1H‐cyclopentafuranone. Key transformations included enzymatic desymmetrization, epimerization, and diastereoselective Grignard addition. Notably, during the synthesis of plasmodiophorol C, we also generated and structurally characterized a previously unknown compound. These synthetic routes provide reliable access to plasmodiophorols A and C, offering valuable standards for probing their biosynthetic pathways and facilitating their identification in marine biomasses. This work lays the foundation for further chemical and biological studies of these unique oxylipins.
item.content:encoded:
The first total syntheses of plasmodiophorols A and C, recently identified α-linolenic acid-derived oxylipins, were achieved from chiral cyclopentafuranone in 12–13 steps. The routes use enzymatic desymmetrization, epimerization, and diastereoselective Grignard addition, providing stereocontrolled access to the natural products and reliable standards for biosynthetic and biological studies.


In 2021, novel oxylipins plasmodiophorol A–C were identified as products of the hydroperoxide bicyclase activity of CYP50918A1 from Plasmodiophora brassicae (Rhizaria, SAR). Here, we report the first total syntheses of plasmodiophorol A and C in 12 and 13 steps, respectively, starting from chiral tetrahydro-1H-cyclopentafuranone. Key transformations included enzymatic desymmetrization, epimerization, and diastereoselective Grignard addition. Notably, during the synthesis of plasmodiophorol C, we also generated and structurally characterized a previously unknown compound. These synthetic routes provide reliable access to plasmodiophorols A and C, offering valuable standards for probing their biosynthetic pathways and facilitating their identification in marine biomasses. This work lays the foundation for further chemical and biological studies of these unique oxylipins.
item.dc:creator: Alexandre Guy, Aurélien Lebrun, Thierry Durand, Camille Oger
item.dc:title: Total Synthesis of Novel Oxylipins: Plasmodiophorols A, C, and Epimers

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Pyridine‐Linked Diarylmaleimide Dimers Bearing Thiophene Chains: Optical and Proton‐Responsive Properties
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Three pyridine‐linked diarylmaleimides bearing thiophene chains were synthesized as a new class of functional dyes. The maleimide dimer framework enhanced the molar absorption coefficients, while longer thiophene chains induced red‐shifted absorption and fluorescence bands. The three compounds also exhibited structure‐specific proton responsiveness and viscosity‐dependent fluorescence.
Diarylmaleimides are promising organic dyes owing to their structural simplicity and the tunability of their electronic structures through aryl substitution. Herein, we designed and synthesized pyridine‐linked diarylmaleimide dimers 1–3 bearing thiophene chains. The pyridine unit imparts proton responsiveness, whereas the length of the thiophene chains controls the electron‐donating ability. To evaluate the effect of the number of maleimide frameworks, h1, a half‐structure of 1, was also synthesized. Comparison of the absorption and fluorescence spectra of 1–3 with those of h1 revealed that the maleimide dimer framework enhances light‐harvesting efficiency and induces larger Stokes shifts. Extending the π‐conjugation through thiophene rings led to redshifted absorption and fluorescence bands, accompanied by increased molar absorption coefficients. As part of their proton‐responsive behavior, acid titration induced larger absorption spectral changes in 1–3 than those observed for h1. Notably, the absorption band of protonated 3 extended into the near‐infrared region. According to 1H NMR analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, 1 exhibited structure‐specific proton responsiveness, involving a conformation in which both the pyridine nitrogen atom and an imide carbonyl oxygen atom participate in proton capture. Additionally, 1–3 displayed viscosity‐dependent fluorescence arising from the restriction of molecular motions.
item.content:encoded:
Three pyridine-linked diarylmaleimides bearing thiophene chains were synthesized as a new class of functional dyes. The maleimide dimer framework enhanced the molar absorption coefficients, while longer thiophene chains induced red-shifted absorption and fluorescence bands. The three compounds also exhibited structure-specific proton responsiveness and viscosity-dependent fluorescence.


Diarylmaleimides are promising organic dyes owing to their structural simplicity and the tunability of their electronic structures through aryl substitution. Herein, we designed and synthesized pyridine-linked diarylmaleimide dimers 1–3 bearing thiophene chains. The pyridine unit imparts proton responsiveness, whereas the length of the thiophene chains controls the electron-donating ability. To evaluate the effect of the number of maleimide frameworks, h1, a half-structure of 1, was also synthesized. Comparison of the absorption and fluorescence spectra of 1–3 with those of h1 revealed that the maleimide dimer framework enhances light-harvesting efficiency and induces larger Stokes shifts. Extending the π-conjugation through thiophene rings led to redshifted absorption and fluorescence bands, accompanied by increased molar absorption coefficients. As part of their proton-responsive behavior, acid titration induced larger absorption spectral changes in 1–3 than those observed for h1. Notably, the absorption band of protonated 3 extended into the near-infrared region. According to 1H NMR analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, 1 exhibited structure-specific proton responsiveness, involving a conformation in which both the pyridine nitrogen atom and an imide carbonyl oxygen atom participate in proton capture. Additionally, 1–3 displayed viscosity-dependent fluorescence arising from the restriction of molecular motions.
item.dc:creator: Takaaki Miyazaki, Honoka Yamaguchi, Akio Mishima, Ryuta Ishikawa, Osamu Hayashida
item.dc:title: Pyridine‐Linked Diarylmaleimide Dimers Bearing Thiophene Chains: Optical and Proton‐Responsive Properties

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Design and Synthesis of Lactose‐Derived Ligands and Their Application in Asymmetric Friedel–Crafts Alkylation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This work describes the synthesis of lactose‐ and glucose‐derived chiral ligands and their application in the catalytic enantioselective Friedel–Crafts alkylation of indole with a series of trans‐β‐nitrostyrenes. The optimal ligand was an acetate protected, lactose‐derived pyridyl imine and its zinc complex afforded ees up to 93% and yields up to 93%. Additionally, a γ,β‐unsaturated‐α‐ketoester was employed in the Friedel–Crafts alkylation with indole, resulting in a 95% ee and 100% conversion.
D‐Lactose is a multi‐tonne waste product from the dairy industry, it is enantiopure and therefore of interest for potential use as a key component in chiral ligands for asymmetric catalysis. Owing to the development of successful carbohydrate‐containing ligands in the past, we now report the development of a series of bi‐ and tridentate lactose‐derived ligands with a variety of protecting groups, all of which are accessible in 3–5 synthetic steps. This is a novel application of lactose, as most carbohydrate ligands applied in metal‐catalysed asymmetric transformations are monosaccharides and none contain a lactose moiety. The lactose‐derived ligands prepared were applied in the asymmetric Friedel–Crafts alkylation of indole with various trans‐β‐nitrostyrenes affording ees up to 93% and yields up to 93%. Additionally, a γ,β‐unsaturated‐α‐ketoester was employed in the Friedel–Crafts alkylation with indole, resulting in a 95% ee and 100% conversion. To probe the functionality of the galactose unit of the lactose ligand, we applied an analogous glucose ligand in the same transformation furnishing inferior results of up to 82% ee and up to 66% yield. This demonstrates the advantage of using a disaccharide ligand.
item.content:encoded:
This work describes the synthesis of lactose- and glucose-derived chiral ligands and their application in the catalytic enantioselective Friedel–Crafts alkylation of indole with a series of trans-β-nitrostyrenes. The optimal ligand was an acetate protected, lactose-derived pyridyl imine and its zinc complex afforded ees up to 93% and yields up to 93%. Additionally, a γ,β-unsaturated-α-ketoester was employed in the Friedel–Crafts alkylation with indole, resulting in a 95% ee and 100% conversion.


D-Lactose is a multi-tonne waste product from the dairy industry, it is enantiopure and therefore of interest for potential use as a key component in chiral ligands for asymmetric catalysis. Owing to the development of successful carbohydrate-containing ligands in the past, we now report the development of a series of bi- and tridentate lactose-derived ligands with a variety of protecting groups, all of which are accessible in 3–5 synthetic steps. This is a novel application of lactose, as most carbohydrate ligands applied in metal-catalysed asymmetric transformations are monosaccharides and none contain a lactose moiety. The lactose-derived ligands prepared were applied in the asymmetric Friedel–Crafts alkylation of indole with various trans-β-nitrostyrenes affording ees up to 93% and yields up to 93%. Additionally, a γ,β-unsaturated-α-ketoester was employed in the Friedel–Crafts alkylation with indole, resulting in a 95% ee and 100% conversion. To probe the functionality of the galactose unit of the lactose ligand, we applied an analogous glucose ligand in the same transformation furnishing inferior results of up to 82% ee and up to 66% yield. This demonstrates the advantage of using a disaccharide ligand.
item.dc:creator: Sandra Bulawa, Julia Bruno‐Colmenarez, Patrick J. Guiry
item.dc:title: Design and Synthesis of Lactose‐Derived Ligands and Their Application in Asymmetric Friedel–Crafts Alkylation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Glycomimetic Amines From Biomass‐Derived Cyrene
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
2,3,4‐Trideoxy‐2‐mannosamines, including the unsubstituted one, are accessible as the only endo diastereoisomers by reductive amination of the green solvent Cyrene, derived from pyrolysis of cellulose containing waste biomass. The products are useful chiral synthetic intermediate to nitrogenated glycomimetics.
Cyrene, a low‐cost commercial chiral compound widely available from the pyrolysis of cellulose containing materials including waste biomass, was employed in the preparation of secondary amines. The process exhibited nearly complete stereoselectivity for the d‐manno configured amines (endo amines) within the 2,3,4‐trideoxy hexose structure. Debenzylation of the N‐benzylamine via catalytic hydrogenation provided the first synthesis of the diastereomerically pure primary amine, employable in turn for accessing the same (2S)‐configured endo amines exclusively through reductive amination with aldehydes. This unprecedented access to pure endo amine furnishes a versatile chiral synthetic intermediate to nitrogenated glycomimetics.
item.content:encoded:
2,3,4-Trideoxy-2-mannosamines, including the unsubstituted one, are accessible as the only endo diastereoisomers by reductive amination of the green solvent Cyrene, derived from pyrolysis of cellulose containing waste biomass. The products are useful chiral synthetic intermediate to nitrogenated glycomimetics.


Cyrene, a low-cost commercial chiral compound widely available from the pyrolysis of cellulose containing materials including waste biomass, was employed in the preparation of secondary amines. The process exhibited nearly complete stereoselectivity for the d-manno configured amines (endo amines) within the 2,3,4-trideoxy hexose structure. Debenzylation of the N-benzylamine via catalytic hydrogenation provided the first synthesis of the diastereomerically pure primary amine, employable in turn for accessing the same (2S)-configured endo amines exclusively through reductive amination with aldehydes. This unprecedented access to pure endo amine furnishes a versatile chiral synthetic intermediate to nitrogenated glycomimetics.
item.dc:creator: Debora Pratesi, Marta Rigacci, Francesca Cardona, Camilla Matassini, Andrea Goti
item.dc:title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Glycomimetic Amines From Biomass‐Derived Cyrene

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Driven Persulfate Activation by g‐C3N4 for the Synthesis of 3‐Substituted‐2H‐Benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐Ones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A metal‐free, visible‐light photocatalytic strategy for synthesizing 3‐substituted‐2H‐benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐ones from aminoarenes and α‐oxocarboxylic acids is presented. Using persulfate and g‐C3N4, a one‐pot two‐step process proceeds through α‐imino acid formation followed by intramolecular dehydrogenative lactonization via a radical pathway. The method operates under mild conditions, is scalable, catalyst‐recoverable, and demonstrates applicability in natural product synthesis.
In both industry and academia, the usage of readily available reagents and heterogeneous catalysts is crucial because they reduce labour and manufacturing wastes. Using persulfate (S2O82−) and graphitic carbon nitride (g‐C3N4) visible‐light photocatalysis, we herein describe a novel and one‐pot, two‐step synthetic methodology for the synthesis of a series of 3‐substituted‐2H‐benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐ones from aminoarenes and α‐oxocarboxylic acids. A radical pathway mechanism for the reaction has also been proposed based on control experiments. The condensation of aminoarenes with α‐oxocarboxylic acids produced the α‐imino acid, which was then subjected to intramolecular dehydrogenative lactonization in situ to provide the desired 3‐substituted‐2H‐benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐ones under the condition employed. Notable advantages of this method include the absence of metal‐ions, mild reaction conditions, gram‐scale applicability, suitability for natural product synthesis (e.g., cephalandole A), catalyst recoverability, and environmental friendliness.
item.content:encoded:
A metal-free, visible-light photocatalytic strategy for synthesizing 3-substituted-2H-benzo[b][1,4]oxazin-2-ones from aminoarenes and α-oxocarboxylic acids is presented. Using persulfate and g-C3N4, a one-pot two-step process proceeds through α-imino acid formation followed by intramolecular dehydrogenative lactonization via a radical pathway. The method operates under mild conditions, is scalable, catalyst-recoverable, and demonstrates applicability in natural product synthesis.


In both industry and academia, the usage of readily available reagents and heterogeneous catalysts is crucial because they reduce labour and manufacturing wastes. Using persulfate (S2O8 2 −) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) visible-light photocatalysis, we herein describe a novel and one-pot, two-step synthetic methodology for the synthesis of a series of 3-substituted-2H-benzo[b][1,4]oxazin-2-ones from aminoarenes and α-oxocarboxylic acids. A radical pathway mechanism for the reaction has also been proposed based on control experiments. The condensation of aminoarenes with α-oxocarboxylic acids produced the α-imino acid, which was then subjected to intramolecular dehydrogenative lactonization in situ to provide the desired 3-substituted-2H-benzo[b][1,4]oxazin-2-ones under the condition employed. Notable advantages of this method include the absence of metal-ions, mild reaction conditions, gram-scale applicability, suitability for natural product synthesis (e.g., cephalandole A), catalyst recoverability, and environmental friendliness.
item.dc:creator: Palani Natarajan, Pooja, Vipan Singh, Reena Choudhary, Riya Badhwar, Khushmeen Kaur
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Driven Persulfate Activation by g‐C3N4 for the Synthesis of 3‐Substituted‐2H‐Benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐Ones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Metal‐Ligand Cooperative Cobalt (II) Catalysts for Synthesis of Triaryl Pyrazolines and Bis(indolyl)methanes via Alcohol Dehydrogenation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
In this study, the cobalt‐catalyzed synthesis of pyrazolines and bis(indolyl)methanes via dehydrogenation of primary alcohols was examined. The control experiments and detection of key intermediates via UV–vis, high resolution mass spectrometry, and IR spectroscopy indicated the involvement of the metal ligand cooperative approach.
In this study, the catalytic activity of cobalt complexes (Cat‐1 and Cat‐2) based on tetrazine ligand was examined for the synthesis of 1,3,5‐triaryl‐substituted 2‐pyrazolines via a multicomponent strategy and the coupling of alcohols with indoles to produce bis(indolyl)methanes (BIMs). The method utilizes alcohols as green alkylating agents under dehydrogenative conditions, offering a sustainable and atom‐economical approach. The methodology exhibits a wide substrate scope of 82 examples showing excellent functional group tolerance, including 13 bioactive compounds. Control experiments studies combined with the detection of key intermediates through UV–vis, fourier‐transform infrared spectroscopy, and high resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) suggested the involvement of metal‐ligand cooperation in the plausible reaction pathway.
item.content:encoded:
In this study, the cobalt-catalyzed synthesis of pyrazolines and bis(indolyl)methanes via dehydrogenation of primary alcohols was examined. The control experiments and detection of key intermediates via UV–vis, high resolution mass spectrometry, and IR spectroscopy indicated the involvement of the metal ligand cooperative approach.


In this study, the catalytic activity of cobalt complexes (Cat-1 and Cat-2) based on tetrazine ligand was examined for the synthesis of 1,3,5-triaryl-substituted 2-pyrazolines via a multicomponent strategy and the coupling of alcohols with indoles to produce bis(indolyl)methanes (BIMs). The method utilizes alcohols as green alkylating agents under dehydrogenative conditions, offering a sustainable and atom-economical approach. The methodology exhibits a wide substrate scope of 82 examples showing excellent functional group tolerance, including 13 bioactive compounds. Control experiments studies combined with the detection of key intermediates through UV–vis, fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and high resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) suggested the involvement of metal-ligand cooperation in the plausible reaction pathway.
item.dc:creator: Rahul Chauhan, Prashant Kukreti, Sheela Kumari, Kaushik Ghosh
item.dc:title: Metal‐Ligand Cooperative Cobalt (II) Catalysts for Synthesis of Triaryl Pyrazolines and Bis(indolyl)methanes via Alcohol Dehydrogenation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Direct Method to Prepare Zwitterionic Organotrifluoroborates: Ammonium and Sulfonium Derivatives
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Organotrifluoroborates find applications in synthesis, diagnostics, and theranostics. This study outlines a one‐step reaction for producing ammonium and sulfonium trifluoroborate zwitterion derivatives. This method achieves satisfactory yields without the need for expensive reagents. The sulfonium derivative shows great stability, and it has been successfully applied to click‐chemistry reactions, demonstrating its practical usefulness.
Organotrifluoroborates are an important functional group that is used in synthesis, diagnostics, and, more recently, in theranostics. Here, we present a practical, one‐step synthesis of ammonium and sulfonium trifluoroborate zwitterionic derivatives by N‐ or S‐ alkylation of bromomethyltrifluoroborate. The reaction proceeds smoothly to produce the desired compounds with good to excellent yields. In particular, the preparation of some sulfonium trifluoroborate derivatives, which avoids the use of expensive or troublesome reagents, their complete spectroscopic characterization, and an example of their application in a click‐chemistry reaction are reported.
item.content:encoded:
Organotrifluoroborates find applications in synthesis, diagnostics, and theranostics. This study outlines a one-step reaction for producing ammonium and sulfonium trifluoroborate zwitterion derivatives. This method achieves satisfactory yields without the need for expensive reagents. The sulfonium derivative shows great stability, and it has been successfully applied to click-chemistry reactions, demonstrating its practical usefulness.


Organotrifluoroborates are an important functional group that is used in synthesis, diagnostics, and, more recently, in theranostics. Here, we present a practical, one-step synthesis of ammonium and sulfonium trifluoroborate zwitterionic derivatives by N- or S- alkylation of bromomethyltrifluoroborate. The reaction proceeds smoothly to produce the desired compounds with good to excellent yields. In particular, the preparation of some sulfonium trifluoroborate derivatives, which avoids the use of expensive or troublesome reagents, their complete spectroscopic characterization, and an example of their application in a click-chemistry reaction are reported.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Re, Antonio Lucchini, Diego Caprioglio, Alberto Minassi, Zhenwei Yuan, Daniela Imperio, Luigi Panza
item.dc:title: A Direct Method to Prepare Zwitterionic Organotrifluoroborates: Ammonium and Sulfonium Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: 1,2,6‐Thiadiazin‐4‐ones: Robust Photocatalysts for the Green Aerobic Oxidation of Sulfides to Sulfoxides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A new photocatalyst for the aerobic photochemical oxidation of sulfides to sulfoxides is described under really low catalyst loading (0.01 mol%) and methanol as the green solvent.
The chemoselective oxidation of sulfides to sulfoxides is accomplished using a new family of photocatalysts, namely 1,2,6‐thiadiazin‐4‐ones. Utilizing a low catalyst loading (0.01 mol%), a 440 nm lamp as the irradiation source, and molecular oxygen as the oxidant, this rapid method is applicable to a broad range of alkyl‐aryl, dialkyl, and diaryl sulfides. Additionally, the method demonstrates practical relevance for the industrially significant synthesis of pharmaceutical compounds, including modafinil and sulforaphane.
item.content:encoded:
A new photocatalyst for the aerobic photochemical oxidation of sulfides to sulfoxides is described under really low catalyst loading (0.01 mol%) and methanol as the green solvent.


The chemoselective oxidation of sulfides to sulfoxides is accomplished using a new family of photocatalysts, namely 1,2,6-thiadiazin-4-ones. Utilizing a low catalyst loading (0.01 mol%), a 440 nm lamp as the irradiation source, and molecular oxygen as the oxidant, this rapid method is applicable to a broad range of alkyl-aryl, dialkyl, and diaryl sulfides. Additionally, the method demonstrates practical relevance for the industrially significant synthesis of pharmaceutical compounds, including modafinil and sulforaphane.
item.dc:creator: Charikleia I. Karaousta, Stamatis K. Serviou, Chrysanthi K. Spyropoulou, Nikolaos Stavridis, Andreas S. Kalogirou, Panayiotis A. Koutentis, Christoforos G. Kokotos
item.dc:title: 1,2,6‐Thiadiazin‐4‐ones: Robust Photocatalysts for the Green Aerobic Oxidation of Sulfides to Sulfoxides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Pd‐Catalyzed Allylic Arylation for Divergent Access to CF3‐Functionalized Fluorenes and Dibenzofulvenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we describe a unified Pd‐catalyzed allylic arylation cyclization strategy that enables the divergent synthesis of CF3‐functionalized fluorenes and dibenzofulvenes from CF3–π‐allyl–Pd complexes.
We report a unified palladium‐catalyzed allylic arylation strategy for the divergent synthesis of CF3‐functionalized fluorenes and dibenzofulvenes from CF3–π‐allyl‐Pd complexes. One pathway features the first α‐selective nucleophilic addition in CF3–π‐allyl‐Pd chemistry to yield CF3‐fluorenes, while the other follows intramolecular Tsuji–Trost allylation and isomerization to generate CF3‐dibenzofulvenes.
item.content:encoded:
Herein, we describe a unified Pd-catalyzed allylic arylation cyclization strategy that enables the divergent synthesis of CF3-functionalized fluorenes and dibenzofulvenes from CF3–π-allyl–Pd complexes.


We report a unified palladium-catalyzed allylic arylation strategy for the divergent synthesis of CF3-functionalized fluorenes and dibenzofulvenes from CF3–π-allyl-Pd complexes. One pathway features the first α-selective nucleophilic addition in CF3–π-allyl-Pd chemistry to yield CF3-fluorenes, while the other follows intramolecular Tsuji–Trost allylation and isomerization to generate CF3-dibenzofulvenes.
item.dc:creator: Rami Sateesh, Chiliveru Priyanka, Ramesh Vislavath, Krishna Swaroop Desireddy, Nagender Punna
item.dc:title: Pd‐Catalyzed Allylic Arylation for Divergent Access to CF3‐Functionalized Fluorenes and Dibenzofulvenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of Axially Chiral Heterobiaryl N‐Oxides via Peptide‐Catalyzed Aldol Reaction
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Resin‐supported tripeptide achieved the dynamic kinetic resolution in the catalytic aldol reaction of formylated heterobiaryl N‐oxides with high efficiency and enantioselectivity. The system features a broad substrate scope and a recyclable catalyst.
A dynamic kinetic resolution of axially chiral heterobiaryl N‐oxides has been developed via an asymmetric aldol reaction catalyzed by a resin‐supported d‐Pro‐Tyr‐Phe tripeptide. This approach provides a pathway to enantioenriched heterobiaryl N‐oxide derivatives possessing both axial and central chirality, affording products with up to 96% ee and 9:1 dr. The methodology demonstrates a broad and robust substrate scope, tolerating a variety of electron‐donating and electron‐withdrawing substituents. In addition, the immobilized catalyst exhibits recyclability for at least three cycles. This system was also applicable to the kinetic resolution of racemic axially chiral substrates.
item.content:encoded:
Resin-supported tripeptide achieved the dynamic kinetic resolution in the catalytic aldol reaction of formylated heterobiaryl N-oxides with high efficiency and enantioselectivity. The system features a broad substrate scope and a recyclable catalyst.


A dynamic kinetic resolution of axially chiral heterobiaryl N-oxides has been developed via an asymmetric aldol reaction catalyzed by a resin-supported d-Pro-Tyr-Phe tripeptide. This approach provides a pathway to enantioenriched heterobiaryl N-oxide derivatives possessing both axial and central chirality, affording products with up to 96% ee and 9:1 dr. The methodology demonstrates a broad and robust substrate scope, tolerating a variety of electron-donating and electron-withdrawing substituents. In addition, the immobilized catalyst exhibits recyclability for at least three cycles. This system was also applicable to the kinetic resolution of racemic axially chiral substrates.
item.dc:creator: Jiaqi Tian, Isao Yoshikawa, Akihiro Sakama, Kazuaki Kudo
item.dc:title: Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of Axially Chiral Heterobiaryl N‐Oxides via Peptide‐Catalyzed Aldol Reaction

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Practical and Flexible De Novo Synthesis of Deoxygenated Carbasugars and Their Cyclitol Epoxides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A de novo synthesis route comprising enantioselective Brown crotylation and ring‐closing metathesis as the key steps enables the synthesis of a‐ and b‐carba‐paratose, a‐ and b‐carba‐colitose as well as four configurational deoxycyclophellitol isosteres.
Literature on carbasugar and cyclitol epoxide glycomimetics is largely biased towards fully hydroxylated cyclitol structures. Partially deoxygenated sugars of varying configuration are widespread in nature and merit translation into the corresponding carbasugars and cyclitol epoxides as well. For this purpose, we developed a de novo synthesis route comprising enantioselective Brown crotylation and ring‐closing metathesis as the key steps and enabling the synthesis of α‐ and β‐carba‐paratose, α‐ and β‐carba‐colitose as well as four configurational deoxycyclophellitol isosteres in a strategy that allows the synthesis of other configurational isomers.
item.content:encoded:
A de novo synthesis route comprising enantioselective Brown crotylation and ring-closing metathesis as the key steps enables the synthesis of a- and b-carba-paratose, a- and b-carba-colitose as well as four configurational deoxycyclophellitol isosteres.


Literature on carbasugar and cyclitol epoxide glycomimetics is largely biased towards fully hydroxylated cyclitol structures. Partially deoxygenated sugars of varying configuration are widespread in nature and merit translation into the corresponding carbasugars and cyclitol epoxides as well. For this purpose, we developed a de novo synthesis route comprising enantioselective Brown crotylation and ring-closing metathesis as the key steps and enabling the synthesis of α- and β-carba-paratose, α- and β-carba-colitose as well as four configurational deoxycyclophellitol isosteres in a strategy that allows the synthesis of other configurational isomers.
item.dc:creator: Yevhenii Radchenko, Nick D. F. Puijmbroeck, Sanne M. Verduin, Jeroen D. C. Codée, Herman S. Overkleeft
item.dc:title: A Practical and Flexible De Novo Synthesis of Deoxygenated Carbasugars and Their Cyclitol Epoxides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Furo‐/Thieno[2,3‐c]pyridine Derivatives: Synthesis, Reactions, and Applications
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Furo‐/thienopyridines, as fused heterocyclic pyridine derivatives, have attracted increasing interest in modern organic chemistry due to a growing number of pharmaceutical and material science studies. Accordingly, substantial progress has been achieved in the development of efficient and versatile synthetic methodologies for their construction. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the synthetic strategies for furo‐/thieno[2,3‐c]pyridines and their derivatives, while also highlighting their fields of application.
Furo‐/thienopyridines constitute a class of heterocyclic compounds that have garnered significant attention in medicinal chemistry due to their diverse biological activities and potential therapeutic applications. These compounds represent a versatile class that, in addition to their importance in medicinal chemistry, also have significant implications in materials science. Ongoing research into their synthesis and functionalization is essential for unlocking their full potential. This review features synthetic approaches to the preparation of furo‐/thienopyridines published from 2008 until 2025, with a primary focus on the [2,3‐c] fusion. Older reports will be mentioned where appropriate for comparison. The characteristics and applications of the described compounds are discussed and summarized in tables.
item.content:encoded:

Accordingly, substantial progress has been achieved in the development of efficient and versatile synthetic methodologies for their construction. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the synthetic strategies for furo-/thieno[2,3-c]pyridines and their derivatives, while also highlighting their fields of application.


Furo-/thienopyridines constitute a class of heterocyclic compounds that have garnered significant attention in medicinal chemistry due to their diverse biological activities and potential therapeutic applications. These compounds represent a versatile class that, in addition to their importance in medicinal chemistry, also have significant implications in materials science. Ongoing research into their synthesis and functionalization is essential for unlocking their full potential. This review features synthetic approaches to the preparation of furo-/thienopyridines published from 2008 until 2025, with a primary focus on the [2,3-c] fusion. Older reports will be mentioned where appropriate for comparison. The characteristics and applications of the described compounds are discussed and summarized in tables.

item.dc:title: Furo‐/Thieno[2,3‐c]pyridine Derivatives: Synthesis, Reactions, and Applications

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: QuinoxP*: A Privileged Ligand in Asymmetric Transition‐Metal Catalysis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review provides a comprehensive overview of the development and applications of the P‐stereogenic ligand QuinoxP*, covering a wide range of transition‐metal‐catalyzed reactions (Rh, Cu, Pd, Ni, Co, Ir, Ag, and Fe), thus demonstrating the exceptionally strong chiral control ability of QuinoxP* and related ligands.
QuinoxP*, characterized by a quinoxaline backbone bearing two P‐stereogenic phosphine units, demonstrates excellent activity and stereoselectivity in asymmetric transition‐metal catalysis. Since its initial synthesis in 2005, and especially over the past decade, there has been a notable increase in studies reporting its application in transition‐metal‐catalyzed asymmetric transformations. Despite this expanding body of literature, a comprehensive review dedicated to this privileged ligand remains absent. To address this gap, the present review provides a thorough overview of the origin of QuinoxP* and its structural features and key properties, as well as its diverse catalytic applications in the past decade. Furthermore, we examine its current limitations and discuss future directions for the advancement and utilization of this remarkable ligand.
item.content:encoded:
This review provides a comprehensive overview of the development and applications of the P-stereogenic ligand QuinoxP*, covering a wide range of transition-metal-catalyzed reactions (Rh, Cu, Pd, Ni, Co, Ir, Ag, and Fe), thus demonstrating the exceptionally strong chiral control ability of QuinoxP* and related ligands.


QuinoxP*, characterized by a quinoxaline backbone bearing two P-stereogenic phosphine units, demonstrates excellent activity and stereoselectivity in asymmetric transition-metal catalysis. Since its initial synthesis in 2005, and especially over the past decade, there has been a notable increase in studies reporting its application in transition-metal-catalyzed asymmetric transformations. Despite this expanding body of literature, a comprehensive review dedicated to this privileged ligand remains absent. To address this gap, the present review provides a thorough overview of the origin of QuinoxP* and its structural features and key properties, as well as its diverse catalytic applications in the past decade. Furthermore, we examine its current limitations and discuss future directions for the advancement and utilization of this remarkable ligand.
item.dc:creator: Jinbao Wang, Yuyang Liao, Chun Wang, Yumei Xiao, Cheng Zhang
item.dc:title: QuinoxP*: A Privileged Ligand in Asymmetric Transition‐Metal Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: [3 + 2] Cycloaddition of (Diazomethyl)dialkylphosphine Oxide and Aryldiazonium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Silver‐catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition of aryldiazonium salts and (diazomethyl)dimethylphosphine oxide is developed as an approach to 2,5‐disubstituted tetrazoles bearing a P(O)(Alkyl)2 group. The method has wide scope and low susceptibility toward electronic and steric effects in the diazonium salt component.
A convenient approach to 2,5‐disubstituted tetrazoles bearing a dialkylphosphine oxide substituent at the C‐5 position is developed. The method involves silver‐catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition of (diazomethyl)dialkylphosphine oxide and aryl diazonium salts. The proposed reaction is compatible with various substituents at the aryl ring and has low susceptibility to their electronic and steric effects. Several heterocyclic diazonium salts were also involved into the transformation successfully. The proposed reaction pathway may include synchronous [3 + 2] cycloaddition or two‐step formation of the tetrazole ring starting with electrophilic attack of aryl diazonium salt at the diazoalkane carbon atom.
item.content:encoded:
Silver-catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition of aryldiazonium salts and (diazomethyl)dimethylphosphine oxide is developed as an approach to 2,5-disubstituted tetrazoles bearing a P(O)(Alkyl)2 group. The method has wide scope and low susceptibility toward electronic and steric effects in the diazonium salt component.


A convenient approach to 2,5-disubstituted tetrazoles bearing a dialkylphosphine oxide substituent at the C-5 position is developed. The method involves silver-catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition of (diazomethyl)dialkylphosphine oxide and aryl diazonium salts. The proposed reaction is compatible with various substituents at the aryl ring and has low susceptibility to their electronic and steric effects. Several heterocyclic diazonium salts were also involved into the transformation successfully. The proposed reaction pathway may include synchronous [3 + 2] cycloaddition or two-step formation of the tetrazole ring starting with electrophilic attack of aryl diazonium salt at the diazoalkane carbon atom.
item.dc:creator: Ilona Tarasiuk, Evgeniy Y. Slobodyanyuk, Dmytro O. Sibgatulin, Oleksandr O. Grygorenko
item.dc:title: [3 + 2] Cycloaddition of (Diazomethyl)dialkylphosphine Oxide and Aryldiazonium Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Dearomatization with Axial‐to‐Central Chirality Conversion
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Atropisomers are exciting synthetic targets with unique properties, which find applications in many fields of chemistry. However, considering them as substrates is not very frequent, notably in the context of dearomatization reaction. We wish to discuss the different strategies for the dearomatization of aromatic atropisomers with axial‐to‐central chirality conversion to afford high‐value compounds that are difficult to access via other synthetic routes.
Aromatic atropisomers, that is to say aromatic compounds that exhibit chirality because of a hindered rotation around the bond linking them to one of their substituents, have recently received an increased interest, both as synthetic targets and as molecules of interest for applications as catalysts or in medicinal chemistry. However, despite the easier access to diversified enantioenriched aromatic atropisomers, considering them as substrates is not very frequent, notably in the context of dearomatization reactions. It is even more regrettable, as such transformations can deliver enantioenriched original motifs through an axial‐to‐central chirality conversion. The goal of this review is to provide a general overview of dearomative transformations of aromatic atropisomers to help the synthetic chemists community identifying new potential research directions in this subfield.
item.content:encoded:

However, considering them as substrates is not very frequent, notably in the context of dearomatization reaction. We wish to discuss the different strategies for the dearomatization of aromatic atropisomers with axial-to-central chirality conversion to afford high-value compounds that are difficult to access via other synthetic routes.


Aromatic atropisomers, that is to say aromatic compounds that exhibit chirality because of a hindered rotation around the bond linking them to one of their substituents, have recently received an increased interest, both as synthetic targets and as molecules of interest for applications as catalysts or in medicinal chemistry. However, despite the easier access to diversified enantioenriched aromatic atropisomers, considering them as substrates is not very frequent, notably in the context of dearomatization reactions. It is even more regrettable, as such transformations can deliver enantioenriched original motifs through an axial-to-central chirality conversion. The goal of this review is to provide a general overview of dearomative transformations of aromatic atropisomers to help the synthetic chemists community identifying new potential research directions in this subfield.

item.dc:title: Dearomatization with Axial‐to‐Central Chirality Conversion

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Elucidation of the Conformational Rate Processes of the Macrocyclic Lactone (–)‐Nonenolide Through Variable Temperature NMR, Quantum Mechanics, and Molecular Dynamics
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The conformational rate processes that influence the NMR spectra of the ten‐membered lactone (–)‐nonenolide are elucidated using VT‐NMR, QM calculations and MD simulations. Reorientation of the alkene moiety with respect to the ring is identified as the slow exchange process. Satisfactory chemical shift predictions required a molecular dynamics‐based analysis of the conformer populations in solution.
Variable‐temperature NMR spectra of the ten‐membered lactone (–)‐nonenolide reveal that the molecule undergoes a hindered conformational interconversion process, which appears to be characteristic of this class of natural products. Using a combination of quantum mechanics (QM) calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, we show that the hindered rate process involves a ‘jump‐rope rotation’ that reorients the endocyclic alkene moiety with respect to the rest of the ring. Quantum mechanical calculations provide reasonably accurate predictions of the interconverting species’ chemical shifts, but several discrepancies exist, which we trace to inadequacies in the description of conformer energies in solution. Closer agreement is obtained from an MD‐based analysis of the conformer populations in explicit solvent. This study illustrates some important ways in which solvent effects can influence the accuracy of theoretical chemical shift predictions.
item.content:encoded:
The conformational rate processes that influence the NMR spectra of the ten-membered lactone (–)-nonenolide are elucidated using VT-NMR, QM calculations and MD simulations. Reorientation of the alkene moiety with respect to the ring is identified as the slow exchange process. Satisfactory chemical shift predictions required a molecular dynamics-based analysis of the conformer populations in solution.


Variable-temperature NMR spectra of the ten-membered lactone (–)-nonenolide reveal that the molecule undergoes a hindered conformational interconversion process, which appears to be characteristic of this class of natural products. Using a combination of quantum mechanics (QM) calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, we show that the hindered rate process involves a ‘jump-rope rotation’ that reorients the endocyclic alkene moiety with respect to the rest of the ring. Quantum mechanical calculations provide reasonably accurate predictions of the interconverting species’ chemical shifts, but several discrepancies exist, which we trace to inadequacies in the description of conformer energies in solution. Closer agreement is obtained from an MD-based analysis of the conformer populations in explicit solvent. This study illustrates some important ways in which solvent effects can influence the accuracy of theoretical chemical shift predictions.
item.dc:creator: Ingso Limbu, Liam R. Hunt, Daniel P. Furkert, Margaret A. Brimble, Mary J. Garson, Elizabeth H. Krenske
item.dc:title: Elucidation of the Conformational Rate Processes of the Macrocyclic Lactone (–)‐Nonenolide Through Variable Temperature NMR, Quantum Mechanics, and Molecular Dynamics

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Hofmann Degradation of Asparagine and Glutamine as an Efficient Approach for the Synthesis of Derivatized Lysine Homologs
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A practical and scalable route to C1 and C2 lysine homologs has been developed using Hofmann degradation of N‐protected L‐asparagine and L‐glutamine as the key step. These short‐tether scaffolds enable efficient synthesis of diverse Fmoc‐protected noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) bearing donor, acceptor, redox‐active, and fluorescent residues. The ncAAs were successfully incorporated into peptide hydrogelators by solid‐phase peptide synthesis.
We present a convenient and efficient approach for synthesizing lysine homologs via the Hofmann rearrangement of N‐protected L‐asparagine and L‐glutamine. This method enabled the preparation of a diverse family of noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) bearing side chains with varied properties, including electron‐donating and electron‐accepting abilities, redox activity, and fluorescence. The luminescence properties and redox behavior of these ncAAs were investigated. Furthermore, the newly synthesized Fmoc‐protected ncAAs were successfully incorporated into a hexapeptide sequence using solid‐phase peptide synthesis, thus expanding the library of hexapeptide hydrogelators.
item.content:encoded:
A practical and scalable route to C1 and C2 lysine homologs has been developed using Hofmann degradation of N-protected L-asparagine and L-glutamine as the key step. These short-tether scaffolds enable efficient synthesis of diverse Fmoc-protected noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) bearing donor, acceptor, redox-active, and fluorescent residues. The ncAAs were successfully incorporated into peptide hydrogelators by solid-phase peptide synthesis.


We present a convenient and efficient approach for synthesizing lysine homologs via the Hofmann rearrangement of N-protected L-asparagine and L-glutamine. This method enabled the preparation of a diverse family of noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) bearing side chains with varied properties, including electron-donating and electron-accepting abilities, redox activity, and fluorescence. The luminescence properties and redox behavior of these ncAAs were investigated. Furthermore, the newly synthesized Fmoc-protected ncAAs were successfully incorporated into a hexapeptide sequence using solid-phase peptide synthesis, thus expanding the library of hexapeptide hydrogelators.
item.dc:creator: Adelaide R. Mashweu, François J. De Beer, Jolien Bertouille, Steven Ballet, Charlotte Martin, Ulrich Hennecke, Charlene Marais, Vladimir A. Azov
item.dc:title: Hofmann Degradation of Asparagine and Glutamine as an Efficient Approach for the Synthesis of Derivatized Lysine Homologs

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Enantioselective Synthesis of Ferrocene‐Fused Planar‐Chiral Cyclic Phosphonic Acid Derivatives
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The asymmetric synthesis of planar‐chiral ferrocene‐fused cyclic phosphonic acids (S)‐1 was achieved starting with chiral acetals (–)‐2. Enantioselective introduction of phosphoryl and 2‐hydroxyethyl groups in (S)‐6 followed by cyclization/hydrolysis provided (S)‐1 in enantiomerically pure forms. The structure of (S)‐1 was determined by X‐ray crystallography, and the application of Brønsted acidic (S)‐1a as chiral organocatalysts was examined.
Planar‐chiral ferrocene‐fused cyclic phosphonic acid half‐ester (S)‐1a was prepared in an enantiomerically pure form by the enantioselective transformation. The synthesis started from Kagan's chiral ferrocenyl acetal (–)‐2a and resulted in ferrocene derivative (S)‐6a substituted by a 2‐hydroethyl substituent at the 1‐position and by a (EtO)2P(O)‐ group at the 2‐position. The chiral acetal substituent in (–)‐2a is a powerful chiral directing group, and thus planar‐chiral (S)‐6a was isolated in >99% ee. The base‐promoted intramolecular transesterification/cyclization of (S)‐6 and following selective hydrolysis of the exocyclic ethoxy moiety afforded (S)‐1a in an enantiomerically pure form. The (η5‐C5Me5)Fe and (η5‐C5Ph5)Fe analogues, (S)‐1b and (S)‐1c, were prepared by the similar strategy, but the use of the exocyclic benzyl ester and the palladium‐catalyzed benzyl‐selective hydrogenolysis is essential to complete the synthesis of (S)‐1b/(S)‐1c. Brønsted acidic (S)‐1a‐c were examined as chiral organocatalysts in the direct Mannich reaction between N‐boc‐imine 10 and acetylacetone showing good catalytic activity and modest enantioselectivity.
item.content:encoded:
The asymmetric synthesis of planar-chiral ferrocene-fused cyclic phosphonic acids (S)-1 was achieved starting with chiral acetals (–)-2. Enantioselective introduction of phosphoryl and 2-hydroxyethyl groups in (S)-6 followed by cyclization/hydrolysis provided (S)-1 in enantiomerically pure forms. The structure of (S)-1 was determined by X-ray crystallography, and the application of Brønsted acidic (S)-1a as chiral organocatalysts was examined.


Planar-chiral ferrocene-fused cyclic phosphonic acid half-ester (S)-1a was prepared in an enantiomerically pure form by the enantioselective transformation. The synthesis started from Kagan's chiral ferrocenyl acetal (–)-2a and resulted in ferrocene derivative (S)-6a substituted by a 2-hydroethyl substituent at the 1-position and by a (EtO)2P(O)- group at the 2-position. The chiral acetal substituent in (–)-2a is a powerful chiral directing group, and thus planar-chiral (S)-6a was isolated in >99% ee. The base-promoted intramolecular transesterification/cyclization of (S)-6 and following selective hydrolysis of the exocyclic ethoxy moiety afforded (S)-1a in an enantiomerically pure form. The (η 5-C5Me5)Fe and (η 5-C5Ph5)Fe analogues, (S)-1b and (S)-1c, were prepared by the similar strategy, but the use of the exocyclic benzyl ester and the palladium-catalyzed benzyl-selective hydrogenolysis is essential to complete the synthesis of (S)-1b/(S)-1c. Brønsted acidic (S)-1a-c were examined as chiral organocatalysts in the direct Mannich reaction between N-boc-imine 10 and acetylacetone showing good catalytic activity and modest enantioselectivity.
item.dc:creator: Takehito Ohji, Akinoshin Okazaki, Qiang Liu, Risa Yasue, Kazuhiro Yoshida, Masamichi Ogasawara
item.dc:title: Enantioselective Synthesis of Ferrocene‐Fused Planar‐Chiral Cyclic Phosphonic Acid Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Investigating the Reactivity and Selectivity in Nucleophilic Fluorination of Secondary Alkyl Bromides and Mesylates: Assessment of the Effect of Counterion and Bulky Alcohols
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Hydrogen bonding provided by pinacol or 2‐trifluoromethyl‐2‐propanol enhances the selectivity of nucleophilic fluorination of several secondary alkyl bromides or mesylates.
Synthesis of secondary alkyl fluorides via nucleophilic substitution faces the problem of high competition with E2 processes. This competition depends on the chemical environment of the substrate, the counterion, and the hydrogen bonding to the fluoride ion. Herein, we combined reliable theoretical calculations and experimental studies to probe the reactivity and selectivity of different substrates. The calculations have shown the significance of SN2 versus E2 competition in various chemical environments. In the experiments, we have tested the use of potassium fluoride combined with 18‐crown‐6 and the bulky alcohols pinacol and 2‐trifluoromethyl‐2‐propanol (TBOH‐F3) with several alkyl bromides and mesylates. The combination of tetrabutylammonium fluoride (TBAF) with these alcohols was also evaluated. We have found that potassium fluoride, combined with the crown ether and the bulky alcohols, is more selective than TBAF combined with these alcohols, although TBAF is more reactive. Alkyl mesylates are less prone to the formation of E2 products, and high fluorination yields can be obtained when using KF (potassium fluoride) combined with 18‐crown‐6 and pinacol in acetonitrile solvent. We have also identified 2‐bromo‐1‐phenylpropane as a very challenging substrate. Even when using a high concentration of 1,1,1,3,3,3‐hexafluoro‐2‐propanol in acetonitrile solution, fluorination of this substrate was not achieved.
item.content:encoded:
Hydrogen bonding provided by pinacol or 2-trifluoromethyl-2-propanol enhances the selectivity of nucleophilic fluorination of several secondary alkyl bromides or mesylates.


Synthesis of secondary alkyl fluorides via nucleophilic substitution faces the problem of high competition with E2 processes. This competition depends on the chemical environment of the substrate, the counterion, and the hydrogen bonding to the fluoride ion. Herein, we combined reliable theoretical calculations and experimental studies to probe the reactivity and selectivity of different substrates. The calculations have shown the significance of S N 2 versus E2 competition in various chemical environments. In the experiments, we have tested the use of potassium fluoride combined with 18-crown-6 and the bulky alcohols pinacol and 2-trifluoromethyl-2-propanol (TBOH-F3) with several alkyl bromides and mesylates. The combination of tetrabutylammonium fluoride (TBAF) with these alcohols was also evaluated. We have found that potassium fluoride, combined with the crown ether and the bulky alcohols, is more selective than TBAF combined with these alcohols, although TBAF is more reactive. Alkyl mesylates are less prone to the formation of E2 products, and high fluorination yields can be obtained when using KF (potassium fluoride) combined with 18-crown-6 and pinacol in acetonitrile solvent. We have also identified 2-bromo-1-phenylpropane as a very challenging substrate. Even when using a high concentration of 1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoro-2-propanol in acetonitrile solution, fluorination of this substrate was not achieved.
item.dc:creator: Eloah P. Ávila, Luis F. Resende, Mauro V. de Almeida, Josefredo R. Pliego
item.dc:title: Investigating the Reactivity and Selectivity in Nucleophilic Fluorination of Secondary Alkyl Bromides and Mesylates: Assessment of the Effect of Counterion and Bulky Alcohols

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Isatogens and Their Reactivity Landscape
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Isatogens (3H‐indol‐3‐one N‐oxides) are compact yet highly versatile heterocycles whose embedded N–O functionality imparts distinctive redox behavior and enables skeletal reorganization. Originally encountered as oxidation products in indigo chemistry, isatogens have since evolved into valuable synthetic platforms, granting access to 3‐indolones, pseudoindoxyls, and structurally remodeled heterocycles. This Review surveys their properties, pharmacology, preparation, and modern synthetic applications.
Isatogen (indol‐3‐one N‐oxide) has attracted renewed attention in synthetic chemistry in recent years. First discovered by Adolf von Baeyer in 1881–1882, its potential as a bioactive scaffold and as a versatile synthetic intermediate remained sporadically explored for much of its history. The recent expansion of the literature has highlighted the distinctive features of the isatogen framework, notably the oxidized nitrogen center embedded within an aromatic system, which confer unusual and exploitable reactivity. This Review provides an up‐to‐date and comprehensive account of isatogen chemistry, encompassing both methods for its preparation and its synthetic applications. Recent advances are integrated with earlier work to present a coherent picture of isatogen reactivity across electrophilic activation, redox processes, cyclizations, and functionalization reactions. The Review is organized to cover historical background, reported bioactivities, and synthetic chemistry, with emphasis on preparative strategies and downstream transformations. By consolidating and critically assessing contemporary developments, this Review clarifies established reactivity patterns, underscores their practical relevance, and aims to stimulate further exploration of isatogens as dynamic scaffolds for modern synthetic strategy development.
item.content:encoded:
Isatogens (3H-indol-3-one N-oxides) are compact yet highly versatile heterocycles whose embedded N–O functionality imparts distinctive redox behavior and enables skeletal reorganization. Originally encountered as oxidation products in indigo chemistry, isatogens have since evolved into valuable synthetic platforms, granting access to 3-indolones, pseudoindoxyls, and structurally remodeled heterocycles. This Review surveys their properties, pharmacology, preparation, and modern synthetic applications.


Isatogen (indol-3-one N-oxide) has attracted renewed attention in synthetic chemistry in recent years. First discovered by Adolf von Baeyer in 1881–1882, its potential as a bioactive scaffold and as a versatile synthetic intermediate remained sporadically explored for much of its history. The recent expansion of the literature has highlighted the distinctive features of the isatogen framework, notably the oxidized nitrogen center embedded within an aromatic system, which confer unusual and exploitable reactivity. This Review provides an up-to-date and comprehensive account of isatogen chemistry, encompassing both methods for its preparation and its synthetic applications. Recent advances are integrated with earlier work to present a coherent picture of isatogen reactivity across electrophilic activation, redox processes, cyclizations, and functionalization reactions. The Review is organized to cover historical background, reported bioactivities, and synthetic chemistry, with emphasis on preparative strategies and downstream transformations. By consolidating and critically assessing contemporary developments, this Review clarifies established reactivity patterns, underscores their practical relevance, and aims to stimulate further exploration of isatogens as dynamic scaffolds for modern synthetic strategy development.
item.dc:creator: Jumreang Tummatorn, Somsak Ruchirawat, Charnsak Thongsornkleeb
item.dc:title: Isatogens and Their Reactivity Landscape

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Huisgen Rearrangement as a Strategic Tool for Trifluoromethyl‐Integrated Energetic Materials
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Trifluoromethyl‐substituted energetic materials are developed by integrating C–C bonded 1,2,3‐triazoles with oxadiazoles via Huisgen cycloaddition and other facile routes. The compounds exhibit high densities, notable thermal stability, and low mechanical sensitivity. Experimental heats of formation and performance calculations reveal compound 10 as a promising high‐performing candidate, highlighting an effective design strategy beyond traditional CHNO energetic frameworks.
A series of trifluoromethyl‐substituted energetic materials were synthesized by combining CC bonded 1,2,3‐triazoles with 1,3,4‐ and 1,2,4‐oxadiazoles through the Huisgen cycloaddition and other facile synthetic routes. All the novel compounds were fully characterized by multinuclear NMR, IR, TGA‐DSC, and elemental analysis. Compound 10 was confirmed by 15N NMR spectroscopy, 6 and 11 were confirmed by single‐crystal XRD studies. Both compounds, 6 and 10, exhibit good densities (1.89 and 1.77 g cm−3), thermal stabilities (215 and 157°C), and are insensitive to mechanical stimuli (IS > 40 J, FS > 360 N). The solid‐state heat of formation was experimentally measured by an oxygen bomb calorimeter. Remarkably, compound 10 exhibits excellent detonation performance (VOD = 7308 m s−1, DP = 22.15 GPa), estimated through the EXPLO 5 software, outperforming previously reported trifluoromethyl‐substituted energetic materials to the best of our knowledge. Overall, this work underscores a balanced design strategy and an effective synthetic route for tuning energetic properties, marking progress beyond traditional CHNO‐based frameworks.
item.content:encoded:
Trifluoromethyl-substituted energetic materials are developed by integrating C–C bonded 1,2,3-triazoles with oxadiazoles via Huisgen cycloaddition and other facile routes. The compounds exhibit high densities, notable thermal stability, and low mechanical sensitivity. Experimental heats of formation and performance calculations reveal compound 10 as a promising high-performing candidate, highlighting an effective design strategy beyond traditional CHNO energetic frameworks.


A series of trifluoromethyl-substituted energetic materials were synthesized by combining CC bonded 1,2,3-triazoles with 1,3,4- and 1,2,4-oxadiazoles through the Huisgen cycloaddition and other facile synthetic routes. All the novel compounds were fully characterized by multinuclear NMR, IR, TGA-DSC, and elemental analysis. Compound 10 was confirmed by 15N NMR spectroscopy, 6 and 11 were confirmed by single-crystal XRD studies. Both compounds, 6 and 10, exhibit good densities (1.89 and 1.77 g cm−3), thermal stabilities (215 and 157°C), and are insensitive to mechanical stimuli (IS > 40 J, FS > 360 N). The solid-state heat of formation was experimentally measured by an oxygen bomb calorimeter. Remarkably, compound 10 exhibits excellent detonation performance (VOD = 7308 m s−1, DP = 22.15 GPa), estimated through the EXPLO 5 software, outperforming previously reported trifluoromethyl-substituted energetic materials to the best of our knowledge. Overall, this work underscores a balanced design strategy and an effective synthetic route for tuning energetic properties, marking progress beyond traditional CHNO-based frameworks.
item.dc:creator: Manojkumar Jujam, Navaneet Kumar, Srinivas Dharavath
item.dc:title: Huisgen Rearrangement as a Strategic Tool for Trifluoromethyl‐Integrated Energetic Materials

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis and Reactivity of Functionalized Tetrazenes: An Original Route Toward 1,2,3‐Triazoles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A series of functionalized tetrazenes was synthesized as N‐rich compounds featuring both modular architecture and versatile reactivity. Thanks to various terminal groups, these derivatives could be used for accessing higher polynitrogenated structures such as tetrazenic oligomers or polymers. Harnessing β‐activated tetrazenes led to a novel synthetic route for accessing 1,2,3‐triazoles.
New alkyltetrazene structures with various terminal functional groups (hydroxyl, halogen, amine, hydrazine) basically in β‐position were synthesized through the oxidation of their hydrazine precursors as novel ingredients for accessing nitrogen‐densified structures. To synthesize hydrazine‐ and amine‐substituted alkyltetrazenes, synthetic routes involving protection and deprotection steps were designed. In addition, β‐activated alkyltetrazenes were obtained from their diol precursors, and their reactivity was investigated, resulting in a novel synthetic route of 1,2,3‐triazoles according to two complementary approaches. The scope was extended to β‐disubstituted derivatives, whose syntheses were also fully described.
item.content:encoded:
A series of functionalized tetrazenes was synthesized as N-rich compounds featuring both modular architecture and versatile reactivity. Thanks to various terminal groups, these derivatives could be used for accessing higher polynitrogenated structures such as tetrazenic oligomers or polymers. Harnessing β-activated tetrazenes led to a novel synthetic route for accessing 1,2,3-triazoles.


New alkyltetrazene structures with various terminal functional groups (hydroxyl, halogen, amine, hydrazine) basically in β-position were synthesized through the oxidation of their hydrazine precursors as novel ingredients for accessing nitrogen-densified structures. To synthesize hydrazine- and amine-substituted alkyltetrazenes, synthetic routes involving protection and deprotection steps were designed. In addition, β-activated alkyltetrazenes were obtained from their diol precursors, and their reactivity was investigated, resulting in a novel synthetic route of 1,2,3-triazoles according to two complementary approaches. The scope was extended to β-disubstituted derivatives, whose syntheses were also fully described.

item.dc:title: Synthesis and Reactivity of Functionalized Tetrazenes: An Original Route Toward 1,2,3‐Triazoles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Transmembrane Transport Activity and Selectivity of Amide‐Based Macrocyclic Anion Receptors of Different Sizes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Amide‐based macrocycles of varying sizes and acyclic bis‐amides were synthesised and tested for Cl– transmembrane transport activity and selectivity versus OH–, NO3–, and HCO3–. Pre‐organised and smaller fluorinated macrocycles showed the highest rates, while simple bis‐amides mimicking half of the macrocycles had only slightly reduced activity. Furthermore, anion transport selectivity could be tuned by cavity size and aryl electron density.
Synthetic anion receptors can transport anions across lipid bilayer membranes. Many anion transporters have been reported, including macrocyclic compounds. However, there are no systematic studies on how the size of macrocycles impacts their anion transport activity and selectivity. Therefore, we prepared eight amide‐based macrocyclic compounds with ring sizes ranging from 18 to 26 atoms, as well as four bis‐amides. We studied these compounds as anion receptors using chloride titrations and molecular modelling, and as anion transporters using liposomes with various fluorescent probes. Neither the size of the macrocycles nor the affinity for chloride was found to be a determining factor for chloride transport activity; pre‐organisation appears to play a more important role. Fluorination was found to have a clear positive effect on anion transport rates, with the bis‐amides performing surprisingly well compared to the macrocycles. The smallest non‐fluorinated macrocycle exhibited selectivity for chloride over hydroxide and nitrate, whereas the pentafluorophenyl bis‐amide demonstrated effective transport of bicarbonate and nitrate, likely due to anion–π interactions in addition to hydrogen bonding. The insights from this study will shape the design of future anionophores.
item.content:encoded:
Amide-based macrocycles of varying sizes and acyclic bis-amides were synthesised and tested for Cl– transmembrane transport activity and selectivity versus OH–, NO3 –, and HCO3 –. Pre-organised and smaller fluorinated macrocycles showed the highest rates, while simple bis-amides mimicking half of the macrocycles had only slightly reduced activity. Furthermore, anion transport selectivity could be tuned by cavity size and aryl electron density.


Synthetic anion receptors can transport anions across lipid bilayer membranes. Many anion transporters have been reported, including macrocyclic compounds. However, there are no systematic studies on how the size of macrocycles impacts their anion transport activity and selectivity. Therefore, we prepared eight amide-based macrocyclic compounds with ring sizes ranging from 18 to 26 atoms, as well as four bis-amides. We studied these compounds as anion receptors using chloride titrations and molecular modelling, and as anion transporters using liposomes with various fluorescent probes. Neither the size of the macrocycles nor the affinity for chloride was found to be a determining factor for chloride transport activity; pre-organisation appears to play a more important role. Fluorination was found to have a clear positive effect on anion transport rates, with the bis-amides performing surprisingly well compared to the macrocycles. The smallest non-fluorinated macrocycle exhibited selectivity for chloride over hydroxide and nitrate, whereas the pentafluorophenyl bis-amide demonstrated effective transport of bicarbonate and nitrate, likely due to anion–π interactions in addition to hydrogen bonding. The insights from this study will shape the design of future anionophores.
item.dc:creator: Karolis Norvaisa, Hasna Sherin Ummat, Lau Halgreen, Giulia Piras, Alice Giaprakis, Alessio Cataldo, Stephen J. Butler, Giacomo Picci, Claudia Caltagirone, Hennie Valkenier
